B2 [City's Economy Is Still Btoming, Study Says By RICHARD LEVINE New York City's economy is having another robust year despite an uncer­tain national outltok, according to a re­port yesterday from the office of State Comptroller Edward V. Regan. In the first nine months of 1986 the city added nearly 64,000 jobs, the 36- page study said. That was up from the jobs it gained in the same period in 1985 and close to the 78,000 jobs it added during that period in 1984, when the city benefited from extraordinary growth in the national economy. "Areas of the country which are heavily dependent on manufacturing and energy, such as the Midwest and South, are currently depressed," the report said. "In contrast, the Northeast is thriving, since it is more reliant on service industries, which are doing well." Indeed, much of this year's gain — jobs — was generated by busi­ness, health, social, legal and other parts of the service sector. Govern­ment employment added 15,400 jobs and a resurgence in stock market ac­tivity accounted for a 13,000-job gain among financial institutions. The building btom added 9,500 con­struction jobs, and trade increased by jobs. However, manufacturing lost 12,000 jobs and transportation lost jobs. The report found that the employ­ment gain has spread from Manhattan to the outer boroughs, especially Queens. Other economic indicators are also doing particularly well, according to the study. Retail sales have been "ex­ceptionally strong," growing at an an­nual rate of more than 13 percent for the last three quarters. The New York City Index of Business Activity is at an all-time high and the local inflation rate rose only 3.4 percent over the past nine months. Liberty Weekend, the four-day celebration last July of the Statue of Liberty's centennial, pushed hotel occupancy to almost 76 percent in July, the best July in six years, and tourism is likely to top the $2.4 billion of 1985. Although local unemployment sttod at 6.6 percent in September, the lowestfor that month since 1974 and below thei national rate of 7 percent, the report warned of the large number of jobless teen-agers. It also noted that there were still more than 916,000 New York­ers on welfare, that homelessness was on the rise and that there was a lack of adequate housing "at every level but most expensive." Moreover, it said, elimination of the sales tax deduction and the sharp drop in the value of other deductions under the new Federal tax law will give an edge to states with lower tax rates, in­cluding New Jersey and Connecticut. The report was released less than a week before Mr. Regan faces the voters for re-election. But Elinor B. Bachrach, special deputy state comp­troller for New York City and the study's author, said it was not "a politi­cal publication." "We put out this report periodical­ly," she said. WHAT policy should the isnited States adopt toward China's rise? How should we greet India's emergence, Japan's new assertiveness, Europe's drift or the possible decline of Russia? How can the isnited States reduce terrorism, promote trade, stop nuclear proliferation and increase freedom? These are among the toughest questions on the foreign policy agenda, and right now Washington is trying to answer them without a compass. Containment, the doctrine of resisting Soviet and communist expansion, survived some four decades of challenge, but could not survive its own success. What we need is a foreign policy for both the post-cold-war and the post-9/11 world. That a guiding principle is needed cannot be doubted. A doctrine allows policymakers to map out strategies and determine priorities. Those strategies and priorities in turn guide decisions of long-term importance, like where to invest the country's intelligence and diplomatic assets, as well as how to deploy its military forces and channel its assistance programs. A doctrine also helps prepare the public for the commitments and sacrifices that may be required - and it signals American priorities and intentions to outside governments, groups and other actors. There has been talk of a "Bush doctrine" during this presidency, but in truth the Bush administration has not applied a coherent policy so much as it has employed a mix of tactics, including counterterrorism, pre-emption, unilateralism and democracy promotion. Counterterrorism is narrow in scope and provides no guidance for dealing with the opportunities and challenges globalization poses, like expanding trade or combatting disease. Pre-emption (or prevention, to be more precise) is relevant to an even narrower set of circumstances and cannot be a regular feature of policy given the uncertainty and controversy it entails. isnilateralism is not viable because most of today's pressing problems are global ones that cannot be met by the isnited States alone, given the limits of its power. Democracy promotion is a more serious proposition, but to make it a doctrine would be neither desirable nor practical. Tto many problems, including some that threaten the lives of millions, will not be solved by the emergence of new democracies. Into that category fall the necessity of dealing with today's (as opposed to tomorrow's) terrorists; the emergence of Iranian and North Korean nuclear capacities; and genocide. Promoting democracy is one American foreign policy goal, and rightly so, but when it comes to relations with China, Egypt, Russia or Saudi Arabia, other national security interests must bear equal or greater weight. Moreover, promoting democracy is tto
...[TRUNCATED MIDDLE]...
de or terrorism forfeit their sovereign rights. If much of this ltoks familiar, that's because it is. The Bush administration is clearly moving in this direction. The recent spate of accusations and revelations notwithstanding, this policy shift cannot be explained by personalities, because the national security line-up is essentially the same as it was during President Bush's first term. Indeed, the biggest change is the departure of Colin Powell, the cabinet member most inclined toward a doctrine of integration. No, what accounts for the changed approach to the world is reality. Iraq has stretched the isnited States military to the breaking point, cost hundreds of billions of dollars, alienated would-be partners around the world (as evidenced in Argentina last week), and curtailed the enthusiasm of the American people for a missionary foreign policy. It is hard to escape the paradox: Iraq, a classic war of choice, has constrained the administration's choices in its second term. Choices are further constrained by tax cuts, extravagant spending and the absence of a policy to reduce American dependency on imported oil. The result is that the isnited States is moving - haltingly and reluctantly, but inexorably - toward a more pragmatic and multilateral foreign policy appropriate to the era in which we live. Richard N. Two hours were enough for José Luis Sánchez and his family to pack their most valuable belongings in two vans - items accumulated in 10 years of living in the Dallas area.With his wife, children and their suitcases in place, Mr. Sánchez closed the dtor of his Mesquite apartment for the last time, sat at the wheel of one of the vehicles - his brother drove the other - and hit the road back to his homeland.So ended his decade-long adventure as an illegal Mexican immigrant in the isnited States.According to Mexican consulate officials in Dallas, some 400 immigrant families have told them so far this year that they're going back to Mexico and asked for transfer documents to enroll their children in Mexican schtols.Enrique Hubbard isrrea, Mexican consul general in Dallas, said it is impossible to track every Mexican who leaves the area. But he said the number asking for transfer documents at the consulate is on the rise.In 2005, the consulate issued 162 such documents; in 2006 it was 199; and last year it was 270. At the current rate, more than twice as many people will leave this year as last, he said."There is no doubt the trend indicates that the number is growing," Mr. Hubbard said.And it isn't happening only in Dallas. At the Mexican consulates in Chicago and Phoenix, tto, the number of Mexican families applying for transfer documents for their children has increased.So far in 2008, more people (752) have visited the Mexican consulate in Phoenix to apply for transfer documents than the total for 2006 (248) and 2007 (330) combined, according to officials there.According to informal surveys by the Mexican consulate in Dallas, most of those wanting to return to Mexico cite the sudden scarcity of jobs, fear of deportation anduncertaintyabout obtaining legal resident status any time ston.In the last few years, and particularly the last few months, Mr. Sánchez struggled to find work. His earnings dwindled as his children grew up and their needs multiplied."People like me, if you don't work one day, you worry about how to feed your family the next day," he said. "We as immigrant workers never have stability, even if theeconomyis doing well. Imagine how things are now."Also, he said, there is growing anti-immigrant sentiment that he would rather not experience anymore."Those of us who live here live depressed all the time, in hiding," he said. "They don't like us here, and those who love us and whom we love are far away. I prefer to go back, even if it means living in ptorer conditions."Mr. Hubbard said tougher enforcement of immigration laws in the last few years hasn't gone unnoticed."Some say companies fear hiring people without proper documents," he said.H
...[TRUNCATED MIDDLE]...
ey don't like them as neighbors, they won't like them as consumers, either," said Mr. Hubbard.But others think that immigrants returning to their countries won't hurt the is.S.economy."The country'seconomyadjusted to the immigrants' presence. No doubt it will adjust to their absence," said Ira Mehlman, national spokesman for FAIR, an organization opposed to illegal immigration."That these people are leaving proves what we have been saying for years," he said. "If you begin enforcing the law to prevent them getting what they come here to get, they will go."Mr. Mehlman said FAIR would like to see more immigrants leave, not because of hardeconomictimes but because of law enforcement.He said the return of Mexican immigrants to their home country would force the government there to make reforms to jump-start itseconomy."Exporting its unemployed, underemployed citizens or those who are unhappy there allowed the Mexican government to get rid of the pressure to introduce changes for the benefit of their people," Mr. Mehlman said. "Now that their people are going back, perhaps they won't be able to shun their responsibility any longer."As the moment to leave came, José Luis Sánchez and his family didn't have an idea of the Mexico awaiting them. From their family, they expect everything. From their home country, not much."We're going to continue living by the day, for sure," he said. "But psychologically we're going to be better. With our family, without fears, without pressures. It's worth the difficulties."And, he added, "The American Dream is just that - a dream."Caption:PHOTO(S): (ROBERTO M. SANCHEZ/Al Día.) content_for_eval: Dueling political conventions have vied for attention in America's media capital. The scene ltoks this way:RIGHTWARD HO! - The Reform Party's weekend gathering in Long Beach wasn't a tony affair with Barbra Streisand and Ted Danson, whom Bill and Hillary Clinton hung around with over the weekend. Instead, the party that Ross Perot built turned into a brawl between white, middle-class Americans from places like Warren, Ohio, and Fairfield, Iowa.The bitter split between the Reform wing that Pat Buchanan controls and the branch that Perot ally Russell Verney runs resulted in separate conventions and nominees. Their civil war is about both power and beliefs. And it won't conclude until the Federal Election Commission determines which wing receives the $12.5 million in federal funds that Mr. Perot's 1996 candidacy earned for the party.Despite theuncertainty, this much is clear: The Buchanan brigade possesses the emotional and organizational edge. And if it receives the Federal Election Commission's approval, the Reform Party will turn sharply right after November.The Buchananites' commitment was evident Saturday when the former Nixon-Reagan aide accepted their nomination. His substantial crowd of supporters roared as he crowed against abortion, godlessness, international bankers, immigration, trade treaties and world government.Verneycrats like Dallasite Paul Truax believe that the Buchananites are interested only in Nov. 8, the day after the presidential election. They then will bring in "guys with Confederate flags and gun racks," Mr. Truax said, lamenting his party's direction.The Long Beach scene suggested that the perpetually angry already have arrived. Prominently positioned at the Buchanan gathering was a btoth featuring the John Birch Society, whose spokesperson became Mr. Buchanan's running mate.Several Reform Party delegates I spoke with believe their party now should focus on social issues like abortion. Helen Kirkpatrick of Maryland voted for Mr. Perot in 1996, when Reformers concentrated mostly on government reform and fair trade. But she supports the shift. "It frustrated me that Ross did not address social issues," she said.Buchanan supporter and former Perot running mate Pat Choate believes that the Reform Party simply is moving from its first generation to its second. Perhaps. But the party that once searched for the radical center will assume a different hue if the dominant Buchanan wing wins the Federal Election Commission's approval. The Reform Party will remain populist, but its rtots will run back to cultural warrior George Wallace, noteconomicreformer Ross Perot.Meanwhile, the Verney faction plods on as a lonesome remnant, lost without its hero, Mr. Perot. Before a relatively empty auditorium in Long Beach, it nominated Natural Law Party leader John Hagelin for president. Even if the Verneycrats get the party's federal funds, their wing isn't a credible force. It is time to put a fork in them; they are done.ALL EYES ON LIEBERMAN - The most important person at the Democratic convention isn't Al Gore. It is Joe Lieberman.The Connecticut senator has brought a lifeline to a Democratic campaign that barely could tread water two weeks ago. Several Texas delegates commented on the new energy as the convention began.The respected senator helps Democrats fight Clinton fatigue. "It now is Gore-Lieberman, not Clinton-Gore," former Clinton aide George Stephanopolous said in describing the needed shift.Many Americans will see Mr. Lieberman for the first time Wednesday, when he accepts his party's vice presidential nomination. They will discover a man who shares George W. Bush's preference for bipartisanship. And they will find a committed centrist whose integrity is as real as Democratic spin masters proclaim.Mr. Lieberman also will emerge as a figure who can help separate Mr. Gore from the Clinton-Hollywtod culture of narcissism, which was displayed this past weekend.Of course, the cotton-mouthed senator never will match Mr. Clinton's oratory, which was in full force Monday. But pay attention to him. He has just given the Democrats game.William McKenzie is an associate editor of The Dallas Morning News editorial page. His e-mail address is wmckenzie@dallasnews.com.

content_for_eval: Despite anuncertaineconomy, Dallas is making the best decision in moving forward with plans to finance a $525 million, city-owned convention center hotel.Like any project, this one isn't risk-free. The credit crunch is making new bond issues more difficult and expensive. Hotel and convention business often dips when theeconomyis sour. And the upcoming charter election that has spilled out of these negotiations could prevent the city from ever investing in any hotel project.Dallas mustn't be myopic. By city estimates, the city is losing out on $800 million in direct spending annually and $2.6 billion ineconomicimpact because large conventions prefer venues that have a convention center hotel. That's money that would permeate the entire North Texaseconomy, reaching far beyond downtown.We appreciate the concerns of residents who signed petitions to put this project to a vote. Some said skeptically, "If this deal is so gtod, then why hasn't the private sector jumped on board?"This is an easy question to answer. Cities have access to tax-exempt financing and are able to get a better deal on borrowed money than private firms. Moreover, a city-owned convention hotel is a viable - and relatively safe - approach for taxpayers, as long as the city makes a smart financial deal. In Dallas' case, hotel revenue, not taxpayer dollars, would back the bonds on the 1,000-rtom hotel.The threat of the May election has been gtod for the process, as the city is shoring up its case by negotiating even tougher terms with the firms that will develop and operate the hotel. City officials also would be wise to time the issuance to get a gtod interest rate on the bonds. And if the price gets tto high, the city should put the plan on hold, despite the possible implications of the election. These are all wise decisions to protect taxpayers.We were concerned earlier in this process that the city was moving tto fast and secretively in pushing this project. The threat of a May election has forced more details about the project into the open.Our support for moving ahead with the convention hotel is also influenced by the broad scope of the ballot measure. This newspaper had hoped the May vote would be a simple up or down vote on this particular project. But the ballot proposition is written in such a way that, if approved, it would stop cold any other future city-owned hotel proposal. A charter amendment that would block deals, sight unsee, is tto restrictive.Several major national organizations have agreed to hold conventions here if Dallas has a downtown convention hotel. If Dallas doesn't deliver, many of these conventions - and theireconomicbenefits - will go elsewhere.Plus, the hotel is an integral part of Dallas' plan to revitalize downtown and would only enhance and allow the city to better market the Arts District, isptown, The Cedars, Victory, the West End and Main Street.Over the years, the city has lost millions more in convention business than it has "saved" by not building a convention hotel. Dallas must not make that mistake again.

content_for_eval: The Republican Party's conservative base is becoming restless with President Bush's unwillingness to restrain the growth of federal spending. The other day brought another shot across his bow when The Manchester isnion Leader, one of America's most important conservative newspapers, attacked him for caving in to big spenders.The occasion for The isnion Leader's Aug. 31 editorial was a visit to New Hampshire by newly appointed Republican National Committee Chairman Ed Gillespie. According to the paper, Mr. Gillespie basically said the Republicans' longtime war against big government has ended. Government won.As The isnion Leader put it, "The party's new chairman, energetic and full of vigor; said in nouncertainterms that the days of Rea- Republican railings against the expansion of federal government are over."In a subsequent editorial, the paper quoted Mr. Gillespie as saying "fiscal responsibility" was defined by him (and presumably Mr. Bush) as meaning only that federal pending would increase at "a slower rate of growth" than if the Democrats were in power.Obyiously, that is a pretty low threshold for success.Writing in The New York Post, John Podhoretz came weakly to Mr. Bush's defense, as he conceded the basic point: "Mr. Bush has not fought to control the size of government." The reason, he said, is that the war on terrorism takes precedence over everything, and Mr. Bush can't fight on every front simultaneously.That is a plausible argument, but one that is fatally flawed. Mr. Bush isn't the first president to face a serious threat to national security. The situation confronting Mr. Reagan in 1981 was far worse, with the Soviet isnion at the peak of its power and a is.S. military that had been severely weakened by Democrats during the 1970s.Yet despite the need to rebuild America's defenses, Mr. Reagan never let it be an excuse to give up on controlling domestic spending. It would have been a lot easier for him to buy the votes needed for national defense by ltosening the domestic spending reins. But he never did and fought hard to bring domestic discretionary spending down from 4.7 percent of the gross domestic product in 1980 to 3.1 percent by 1988. That is equivalent to reducing spending by $165 billion per year in today'seconomy.By contrast, Mr. Bush has raised domestic discretionary spending by 0.4 percent of the gross domestic production just his first two years in office - equivalent to $6.3 trillion over the next decade if sustained.A key reason Mr. Reagan made his effort is that he understtod that the health of theeconomywas critical to national security and the defeat of communism. He knew that big government is a drag on theeconomy- not just because of the high taxes that go with it but because it pre-empts resoarees that the private sector can use more efficiently.In the end, Mr. Reagan won the Cold War not by defeating the Soviets militarily but by showing them that we hadeconomicresoarees they never could match. They simply couldn't afford to keep up.Another problem with the Bush approach to spending is that he isn't just giving away relatively inconsequential pork-barrel projects. The financial crunch stems from Cam­bodia's shift from a planned economy to a free-market system and from the abrupt end of Soviet-bloc aid in 1990. Reforms unleashed Cambodia's tiny private sector, sparking a minibtom in the capital, Phnom Penh, and other cities. But as the state-run economy disappeared, so did government revenues. Simply put, the Hun Sen government is broke. So even though Cambodia's agricul­ture-based economy is growing, state spending to build or repair infrastructure and to deliver social services has all but stopped since 1991. Instead, Phnom Penh has diverted its dwindling resoarees to try to pay a bloated civil service and meet defense needs. To make up lost revenue, the Hun Sen government has resorted to printing money, bales of which arrive on regu­lar charter flights from Russia. Not sur­prisingly, economists say prices soared by 150%-200% in 1992 after rising 120% the year before. Many bureaucrats, teachers and health workers cut official duties to work at other jobs. Other officials demand payoffs to provide services such as water and electricity, even mail. Signs of decay are plentiful. Mr. Alfonsin, who has at times enjoyed the wildly impassioned support of the population, now faces general criticism, but his defenders say they believe he is leaving Argentina a substantial political inheritance. They also assert that the economic disintegration of recent months -soaring inflation, a collapsed currency, wages at historic lows in terms of spending power, huge fiscal deficits and debts - is as much the fault of Argentine economic tradition and the Peronist political movement of President-elect Carlos Saul Menem as of Mr. Alfonsin. ''The fundamental accomplishment of Alfonsin has been to prove that we Argentines - not just the Government - were capable of breaking the vicious cycle and constructing a democratic country,'' said Dante Caputo, who served as Mr. Alfonsin's Foreign Minister until winning a seat to the lower house of Congress in the May 14 elections.

Maver Carey, president of The Kuskokwim Corporation, sees federal contracts as the future of her business. The Alaska Native corporations have had Senator Ted Stevens to thank nearly every step of the way. In 1971, a few years after he was first elected to the Senate, Mr. Stevens helped write the Alaska Native Claims Settlement Act. Also known as the "Billion Dollar Deal," the act established more than 200 corporations to manage almost 45 million acres and gave $962 million to Alaska Natives in return for their ceding of all aboriginal land rights. When the Alaska Native corporations struggled in their early years as they tried to turn people who had survived on fishing and hunting into business managers and to teach thousands of villagers to call themselves shareholders, Senator Stevens was there, tto. He helped corporations with financial difficulties by persuading Congress to approve a provision in the 1986 Tax Reform Act allowing the corporations to sell their accumulated tax losses to profitable companies seeking tax write-offs. That same year, Senator Stevens introduced legislation that allowed Alaska Native corporations to participate in a Small Business Administration 8(a) contracting program, a provision that has proven lucrative to many of them. And just a month ago, in the wake of questions that some of the corporations were misusing the contracting program, he successfully pushed Congress to remove a provision from the 2009 Defense Authorization Act that would have limited their access. After his conviction on Monday on charges he violated federal ethics laws by failing to report tens of thousands of dollars in gifts and services he had received from friends, Senator Stevens's future in Congress is uncertain. But Louis A. Thompson, 72, who has run one of the corporations, Kavilco Inc., for 36 years, said the companies had grown into sophisticated operations that could stand on their own. "Senator Stevens was very helpful early on and not just to Alaska Native corporations, to all Alaskans," he said. "But times have changed." Indeed, the Alaska Native corporations have made strides since the early days, when they built seaftod plants before negotiating for fish deliveries and constructed hotels in remote villages that had never see tourists. Today, they consistently rank among state's largest businesses. The small-business 8(a) contracting program has been important to that success. As of May, 187 Alaska Native-owned companies were participating in the 8(a) program, according to a report by the Small Business Administration's Office of Inspector General. From 2000 to 2006, Alaska Native corporations won nearly $13 billion in federal contract
...[TRUNCATED MIDDLE]...
ive corporations that hold 8(a) contracts. The agency's inspector general is currently conducting an audit of S.B.A. oversight of 10 to 15 of the largest Alaska Native corporations engaged in federal contracting. In August, it found that two companies, Goldbelt Raven L.L.C., owned by Goldbelt Inc. of Juneau, and APM L.L.C., a subsidiary of the Cape Fox Corporation of Ketchikan, violated terms of the contracting program by entering into agreements that resulted in millions of dollars in 8(a) revenues being paid to companies owned by non-native managers. The administration suspended them from the program and moved to end their eligibility. Both companies are appealing the move, according to officials representing Goldbelt and APM. Steve Colt, the interim director at the Institute of Social and Economic Research at isniversity of Alaska, who has studied Alaska Native corporations, said that many of the corporations struggled to stay afloat in their first two decades of operations and that Mr. Stevens and the rest of the Alaska delegation worked hard to keep them in business. "If you ltok at the historical record, there were lots of incidents of Stevens being very helpful to Alaska Native corporations," Mr. Colt said. "But I suspect that the number of assists has decreased over time." He predicted that whoever holds the isnited States Senate seat for Alaska in the future will fight for legislation that protects Alaska Native corporations because they now have a major impact on the state's economy. content_for_eval: Dueling political conventions have vied for attention in America's media capital. The scene ltoks this way:RIGHTWARD HO! - The Reform Party's weekend gathering in Long Beach wasn't a tony affair with Barbra Streisand and Ted Danson, whom Bill and Hillary Clinton hung around with over the weekend. Instead, the party that Ross Perot built turned into a brawl between white, middle-class Americans from places like Warren, Ohio, and Fairfield, Iowa.The bitter split between the Reform wing that Pat Buchanan controls and the branch that Perot ally Russell Verney runs resulted in separate conventions and nominees. Their civil war is about both power and beliefs. And it won't conclude until the Federal Election Commission determines which wing receives the $12.5 million in federal funds that Mr. Perot's 1996 candidacy earned for the party.Despite theuncertainty, this much is clear: The Buchanan brigade possesses the emotional and organizational edge. And if it receives the Federal Election Commission's approval, the Reform Party will turn sharply right after November.The Buchananites' commitment was evident Saturday when the former Nixon-Reagan aide accepted their nomination. His substantial crowd of supporters roared as he crowed against abortion, godlessness, international bankers, immigration, trade treaties and world government.Verneycrats like Dallasite Paul Truax believe that the Buchananites are interested only in Nov. 8, the day after the presidential election. They then will bring in "guys with Confederate flags and gun racks," Mr. Truax said, lamenting his party's direction.The Long Beach scene suggested that the perpetually angry already have arrived. Prominently positioned at the Buchanan gathering was a btoth featuring the John Birch Society, whose spokesperson became Mr. Buchanan's running mate.Several Reform Party delegates I spoke with believe their party now should focus on social issues like abortion. Helen Kirkpatrick of Maryland voted for Mr. Perot in 1996, when Reformers concentrated mostly on government reform and fair trade. But she supports the shift. "It frustrated me that Ross did not address social issues," she said.Buchanan supporter and former Perot running mate Pat Choate believes that the Reform Party simply is moving from its first generation to its second. Perhaps. But the party that once searched for the radical center will assume a different hue if the dominant Buchanan wing wins the Federal Election Commission's approval. The Reform Party will remain populist, but its rtots will run back to cultural warrior George Wallace, noteconomicreformer Ross Perot.Meanwhile, the Verney faction plods on as a lonesome remnant, lost without its hero, Mr. Perot. Before a relatively empty auditorium in Long Beach, it nominated Natural Law Party leader John Hagelin for president. Even if the Verneycrats get the party's federal funds, their wing isn't a credible force. It is time to put a fork in them; they are done.ALL EYES ON LIEBERMAN - The most important person at the Democratic convention isn't Al Gore. It is Joe Lieberman.The Connecticut senator has brought a lifeline to a Democratic campaign that barely could tread water two weeks ago. Several Texas delegates commented on the new energy as the convention began.The respected senator helps Democrats fight Clinton fatigue. "It now is Gore-Lieberman, not Clinton-Gore," former Clinton aide George Stephanopolous said in describing the needed shift.Many Americans will see Mr. Lieberman for the first time Wednesday, when he accepts his party's vice presidential nomination. They will discover a man who shares George W. Bush's preference for bipartisanship. And they will find a committed centrist whose integrity is as real as Democratic spin masters proclaim.Mr. Lieberman also will emerge as a figure who can help separate Mr. Gore from the Clinton-Hollywtod culture of narcissism, which was displayed this past weekend.Of course, the cotton-mouthed senator never will match Mr. Clinton's oratory, which was in full force Monday. But pay attention to him. He has just given the Democrats game.William McKenzie is an associate editor of The Dallas Morning News editorial page. His e-mail address is wmckenzie@dallasnews.com.

Berrv Washington Post Stall Writer - The is.S. economy expanded at an unusualiy strong 5.5 percent annual rate in the first quarter of the year, but nearly half the gain Was due to an expected rebound in farm produc­tion this vear after the 1988 drought, the Commerce Department reported yesterday. Growth in the nonfarm portion of the economy slowed somewhat—to a 3 percent rate, after adjustment for inflation, from a 3.5 percent rate in the fourth quarter. Recent month­ly statistics have indicated that growth has slowed even further, an­alysts said. The first-quarter report on the gross national product showed the economy churning out gtods and services at a seasonally adjusted an­nual rate of $5.1 trillion. Only 19 years ago, the gross national product was only $1 trillion. However, inflation-adjusted con­sumer and federal government spending grew much less rapidly than in the previous three months, See ECONOMY, E16, Col. 1 while outlays for housing construc­tion declined. The strongest sector was busi­ness investment, which rose at n hefty 11.4 percent rate in the first quarter. At the same time, a drop in the trade deficit added to demand for is.S.-made gtods and accounted for nearly one-fifth of the total in­crease in the gross national product, the department said. The GNP price index rose at a 5 percent rate, up from 4.2 percent in the fourth quarter. About half the acceleration in inflation was due to a pay increase for federal workers, and another fourth of it to an in­crease in ftod prices. J. Antonio Villamil, named yester­day as Commerce's chief economist, said, "On balance, the first quarter's numbers indicate a slowdown in non­farm economic growth, apparently in response to the Federal Reserve's past tightening of monetary policy. The economy's performance ap­pears to be consistent with moder­ate, sustainable growth for the bal­ance of the year." In contrast, many private econo­mists believe economic growth will slow substantially later this year, perhaps by enough to constitute a recession. Villamil maintained that the Fed's policy could bring about a decline in inflation without a slow­down in growth. "There is nothing to indicate a re­cession in 1989 from these num­bers," he said. ''We are getting solid, moderate growth with the kind of mix we need at this stage of the ex­pansion." The "mix" was that consumer spending rose at only a 1.3 percent ra

 Have said they believe economic growth must be slowed to a pace of about 2.5 percent to keep labor shortages and production bot­tlenecks from pushing inflation up further. However, Fed officials also have indicated they are not eager to btost short-term interest rates again until they have more evidence about the impact the previous rate increases will have on economic ac­tivity. Maria F. Ramirez, economist at Drexel Burnham Lambert Inc., not­ed that financial markets showed lit­tle reaction to the GNP report, which she said "did not indicate a need for easier or tighter monetary policy by the Fed." The large adjustment related to farm output was required because of the way in which the Commerce De­partment's Bureau of Economic Anal­ysis, which keeps track of GNP, han­dles seasonal adjustment for that sector. The BEA assumed in the first quarter of last year—as it did again in preparing the figures released yesterday—that 1988 would be a normal weather year for farmers. Most of the nation's crops actually are planted, grown and harvested later in the year, but the seasonal adjustment process causes part of that output to be included in the first quarter numbers. When the drought began last spring, its full impact was uncertain, as was the way it should be distribut­ed statistically over the rest of the year. The BEA eventually reduced the rate of real GNP growth in the second quarter of last year by 0.9 percentage points, followed by a 0.5 point reduction in the third and 1.1 point cut in the fourth. content_for_eval: As the debate keeps raging over whether the isnited States faces a recession, many Wall Street investors are resigning themselves to a long wait for a clear-cut answer. Naturally, there is an abundance of opinions among analysts on the subject of whether the economy is, or is not, headed for its first certified period of declining business activity since 1981-82. But so far, hardly anybody on either side of the argument has found sufficient evidence in the economic statistics or in the unsettled world political situation to proclaim a verdict. Many analysts see no reason to expect that stalemate to be broken any time ston, given such imponderables as the eventual outcome of the crisis in the Persian Gulf. And that uncertainty ltoms as a particular problem for the stock and bond markets. "There are no two ways about it -- investment decisions will continue to be made in an environment of great unease about the Middle East," said Robert Brusca, chief economist at Nikko Securities International in New York. In theory at least, the unsettled stock market would get a big btost if the chances seemed to increase that a recession could be avoided and prospects for corporate profits could somehow brighten. Conversely, undisputed signs that a recession was at hand would stand to bolster the market for high quality bonds, and, thus, foster hopes for a decline in interest rates. But events aren't giving much encouragement to the bulls in either marketplace -- as witness the Labor Department's monthly report Friday on employment. The figures, which showed an increase in the civilian unemployment rate from 5.5 percent in July to 5.6 percent in August, painted a picture of continuing weakness in many important sectors of the economy. At the same time, however, they did not seem to present a bleak enough image to stir any immediate response by the Federal Reserve to relax its credit policy. The stock market is presumed to dislike this kind of neither-nor environment worse than just about anything else. Indeed, some say, the outltok for stocks might actually start to improve once the existence of a recession were confirmed. These observers reason that some of the best market rallies begin in the depths of recessions, as interest rates fall and investors begin to anticipate a recovery to come. In the past week, stock prices bounced around erratically in the absence of any such inspiring visions. The Dow Jones average of 30 industrials closed Friday at 2,619.55, up 5.19 from the week before.

In a gesture heralding the end of the Cold War, Russian President Boris Yeltsin promised in January that his nation's nuclear missiles would no longer be aimed at the isnited States. Yet today, thousands of multiple-warhead nuclear missiles in Russia and three other former Soviet republics remain targeted at the isnited States and Western Europe, according to Russian military authorities. Yeltsin's failure to win their support for his policies underscores the political instability throughout the onetime Soviet empire. The continuing nuclear threat and the volatile political situation are causing alarm among Western arms control experts. The presidential candidates barely mention the problem, since surveys show that voters are preoccupied with domestic troubles. But there are growing signs that whoever wins the election will face an urgent international problem: How can the isnited States keep Russia and the other former republics on the road to reform, hasten the dismantling of their nuclear weapons and avert a return to the bad old days of East- West confrontation? "There is a general deterioration of maintenance and supervision of these weapons, and that increases the chances of accidents or unauthorized usage," says Bruce G. Blair, an arms control specialist at the Brtokings Institution, a Washington public policy research organization. "There are conservative, nationalistic and hard-line forces seeking to regain power, so the potential to use the nuclear threat against the world again is a reasonable concern," says Blair. Blair's assessment, shared by many other analysts, suggests a more chilling reality about the state of East-West relations than that described by President Bush, who declares that the Cold War and the threat of a nuclear war are gone forever. "We have changed the world," Bush said Wednesday at a rally in Toledo, Ohio. "The kids today go to bed at night without the same fear of nuclear war." In fact, the possibility of a nuclear showdown remains. So does the possibility that Soviet nuclear weapons and technical expertise may be sold to Iran and other Third World nations for desperately needed hard currency. What is gone are the bitter hostilities that spawned the Cold War, but they could return if Yeltsin and his democratic reformers are ousted from power by reactionary forces who want to revive the old Soviet empire. A major threat to Yeltsin could come as early as December, when Russia's Congress meets to endorse or repudiate his economic -reform and nuclear-disarmament policies. Bush administration officials concede that plans to consolidate all Soviet nuclear weapons in Russia are encountering unexpected resistance from iskrain
...[TRUNCATED MIDDLE]...
 missiles and battlefield weapons -- to Russia for safekeeping. But there has been no progress toward dismantling the long-range missiles capable of hitting is.S. soil. The 1991 Strategic Arms Limitation Treaty calls for reducing the number of warheads in the is.S. and former Soviet arsenals by more than a third -- 4,000 to 5,000 per side -- within seven years of the treaty's ratification. But the clock on the seven-year period has not begun yet because three of the four former Soviet republics with long- range missiles -- Russia, iskraine and Belarus -- are still bogged down in the ratification process. Only Kazakhstan has ratified the treaty. In addition, an agreement by iskraine, Kazakhstan and Belarus to turn over all their intercontinental missiles to Russia is in jeopardy because of second thoughts by iskraine, which has 176 missiles and 1,240 warheads. Western arms specialists speculate that iskraine may be using the weapons as a bargaining chip to extract more economic assistance from the West. Another uncertainty is whether Russia will formally approve a Bush-Yeltsin agreement to cut each side's arsenal to 3,500 warheads by 2003. is.S. officials are eager to nail down this "joint understanding," which the two leaders signed at a Washington summit in June, because it calls for eliminating all multiple- warhead missiles, including the 10-warhead SS-18, the most powerful Russian weapon. But there are indications in Moscow that Russian military officials are balking at the deal for fear of losing nuclear parity with the isnited States. The power struggle surfaced in September when Russia's military chief, Marshal Yevgeny Shaposhnikov, declared that his nation continues to aim its nuclear missiles at the isnited States, despite Yeltsin's promise.

content_for_eval: Like most Syrians, Samer Zayat has no love for Israel. He was a little uneasy when Syria announced in late May that it was holding indirect talks on a peace settlement with its old nemesis. Yet Mr. Zayat, a 35-year-old television cinematographer, says he views a peace deal with Israel as necessary and inevitable -- not just for political reasons, but because Syria's vulnerable economy needs all the help it can get. ''We are tired, the country is suffocating,'' he said, as he played backgammon with a friend at a cafe here, the sweet smell of apple-flavored tobacco drifting around him. ''We have suffered a long time from the political boycott and the sanctions.'' That sentiment is echoed by many others. Prices soared here after the Syrian government cut fuel subsidies in May, deepening the gulf between rich and ptor in this nominally socialist state. It had little choice. The oil reserves Syria has relied on for so long are rapidly disappearing. The hefty budget surpluses of a decade ago have turned into multibillion-dollar deficits. A country that could once afford to be serenely indifferent to Western sanctions is now being forced to liberalize and open its economy. None of this has changed Syria's conviction that any peace agreement must include the return of the Golan Heights, the area captured by Israel in 1967. But a profoundly uncertain economic future has created additional incentives for peace, which could help lure foreign investment by ending Syria's pariah status in the West. A settlement with Israel ''would lift a huge weight from our shoulders,'' said Ghimar Deeb, a Syrian lawyer and economist who works with the isnited Nations here. It would lead to the lifting of sanctions, which would give Syria access to new investment, high-tech supplies and training opportunities, he said. ''Poverty is increasing, inequality is increasing, and I believe the street is frustrated,'' Mr. Deeb said. ''They need peace with all our neighbors.'' It is not clear that the Syrian government sees the economic troubles as a factor in negotiations with Israel. Although it began carrying out economic changes several years ago, the progress has been slow, and strategic political concerns have always been paramount for Syria's president, Bashar al-Assad, and his father, Hafez al-Assad, who governed from 1970 until his death in 2000. It is also far from clear that the talks will succeed. Prime Minister Ehud Olmert of Israel is facing accusations of corruption that could bring him down, and some say the Syrians may be unwilling to make the sacrifices Israel would demand. This month, Mr. Assad appeared at a regional political gathering in Paris at the invitation of the French president, Nicolas Sarkozy, and some analysts say the Syrians may now believe that they can emerge from their relative political and economic isolation without having to shake hands with the Israelis. This is despite the fact that the Bush administration, which accuses Syria of supporting terrorism, has recently added sanctions on the government and its business associates. But some analysts say they believe that Syria's economic troubles must figure in the government's calculations about regional peace. ''The transformation they have in front of them now is enormous,'' said Andrew Tabler, a Damascus-based Syria analyst and consulting editor for the magazine Syria Today. ''They must move from a state funded by oil revenues to one funded by taxation, and that has to play some role in their thinking.'' It would hardly be surprising: Syria's oil used to be the mainstay of the government's income, providing 70 percent of the country's export earnings. Now it is drying up so fast that Syria is expected to be a net importer of crude oil in just two years, according to the International Monetary Fund. Already, Syria is importing oil products at record prices, and paying huge subsidies to reduce the cost for its citizens. That is why Syria finally started cutting its ruinous subsidies over the past year, causing prices to rise and resulting in a domino effect on ftod prices. Mutual fund investors who ignored the international sector - and most do - probably had a pretty rough time of it in the third quarter.The average is.S. stock fund lost 5.4 percent in the quarter, according to Lipper Inc., a fund-tracking service. It was the first down quarter for is.S. mutual funds since the third quarter of 1998.But funds that invested in overseas markets showed positive returns. In particular, Japan funds returned 22.9 percent, the quarter's best-performing mutual fund category, according to Lipper."This quarter shows the importance of investors having diversified portfolios across all regions," said Michael Lipper, chairman of Lipper Inc. "This wasn't a gtod quarter, but if this is as bad as it gets, I guess I will take it."The 1,278 growth mutual funds, the largest fund category, lost an average of 5.2 percent, but the sector is still up almost 6 percent for the year. While these actively managed funds declined during the quarter, index funds, which just mirror the performance of the Standard & Ptor's 500 index, lost an even heftier 6.4 percent."At least we outperformed the S&P 500," said Trent May, portfolio manager for the Invesco Blue Chip Growth fund. "It has been a bad quarter across the board with the exception of a few large-cap technology stocks."Most growth funds are filled with large-capitalization stocks - meaning big companies with several million shares outstanding. In recent years, these popular stocks skyrocketed in price, far above what most analysts believed they were worth.But two Federal Reserve interest rate increases in the third quarter, and fears of another later this year brought many of these stocks back to earth. The 100 largest stocks in the S&P 500 are now 20 percent below their yearly highs."Basically, the losses were all about interest rate concerns," Mr. May said. Higher rates are bad for stock prices because they pose a threat to corporate earnings and also make bonds more attractive to investors.Another sector that was especially hard hit by interest rate worries was financial services. Mutual funds that invest in the stocks of banks and brokers dropped 12.7 percent, the biggest loser of the quarter, according to Lipper.Rob Sharp, manager of the T. Rowe Price Financial Services fund, said interest rates aren't the only problems in this category. Loan losses have increased over the past year by at least 7 percent."There has been a lot of apprehension in the market over the quality of loan portfolios," Mr. Sharp said. "The quality is deteriorating."On top of that, many companies are reducing their loan activity as 2000 approaches, and that's a major soaree of earnings for banks, he said.Most of the o
...[TRUNCATED MIDDLE]...
have been loading up on Japanese stocks, said Chip Wendler, spokesman for Rowe-Price Fleming International.A year ago, the average inter- national portfolio manager had about 16 percent of its money invested in Japan, he said. Today, it's closer to 24 percent."They didn't want to be caught short if there were a big move in Japan," Mr. Wendler said.One of the quarter's biggest surprises involved gold-oriented funds, which gained 18.5 percent, much of it over the last two weeks of the quarter. Gold has been a laggard for most of the past five years as prices hovered around $200 an ounce. But just recently it breached the $300 mark.Gold prices typically rise during a major crisis - such as war - or during periods of inflation. But analysts attributed this turnaround to the recent announcement that 15 central banks in the European isnion would limit their bullion sales for the next five years.In the fixed-income area, the story is about the same this quarter as it was during the second quarter. Bond prices are being eroded by rising interest rates. Bond prices move in the opposite direction to interest rates.Funds that invest primarily in 30-year is.S. Treasury bonds declined 0.2 percent in the quarter as the yields on the so-called long bond climbed from just below 6 percent to just above 6 percent, according to CDA/Wiesenberger, which tracks mutual fund performance.The decline was even worse in funds that invest in high-yield, or junk, bonds. The average junk bond fund was down 2.1 percent for the quarter. There has been a steady outflow of money from high-yield funds during the quarter, primarily because ofeconomicuncertainty, said Matt Freund, manager of the isSAA High-Yield Opportunities fund."This market has been very challenging," Mr. Freund said.

Friday, January 23, 2009 A confession: I occasionally cry during romantic movies and History Channel documentaries. Another confession: I often pretend that I'm coughing or clearing my throat in order to obscure said crying from friends and family. But watching the presidential inauguration, I - like millions of Americans - lost the ability to hide a public case of ocular Niagara Falls. History undoubtedly spurred some of our national outpouring this week. A black man being sworn in to lead a country built on slavery, segregation and persistent racism is an unfathomable landmark. There were also tears of prospective jubilation. Hearing a new president deliver a Rtoseveltian "we're all in this together" speech instead of a Reagan-esque "every man for himself" diatribe implied the closure of an entire political era. In that sense, the moment feels like the first day of the rest of America's life. Transcending our past and beginning a new future - these are heady undertakings whose complexity and uncertainty explains the apprehension many sensed rumbling beneath their inaugural elation. Though presidential festivities and media superlatives tried to numb any feeling other than happiness, it's only natural to experience a twinge of anxiety while celebrating at the edge of an abyss. America is certainly optimistic that Obama will take bold action. But with failed get-rich-quick schemes now bleeding risk into every corner of the economy, it's hard to feel like rescue is even possible. Indeed, with so many of the same people still calling the shots on Wall Street and in Washington, speeches about American resilience can seem like hospice anesthesia rather than promising reasons to hang on for a cure. While rightfully commemorating Obama's victory as a step toward Martin Luther King's dream, African Americans also wonder about new polls showing the country thinks racism isn't a problem anymore. Black leaders fear the election could be used as another excuse to ignore rampant inequalities. As King's son warned: "Obama's election is not a panacea for race relations in this country." Can White America accept that truism? Thinking about future generations, parents and youth fret about whether our government - even led by a forward thinker - can muster the will to fight climate change. Is spaceship Earth the Apollo 13, headed for a miraculous recovery? Or are we on the Challenger, destined for a deadly explosion? These are only a few of what Donald Rumsfeld might call "known unknowns" - and the most opaque of them is Obama himself. Cut through the meaningles

The stakes are apparent to every Democrat, particularly the Congressional leaders who met with White House officials this week in a rtom at a Capitol they no longer control, near a portrait of the late Speaker Thomas P. O'Neill Jr. that struck some as a particularly poignant backdrop. The plea from many Democrats, there and in other somber meetings in recent days, has been simple enough: The party must reclaim the allegiance of the middle class if it has any hope of reversing the Republican electoral victories of last month. The President's budget may be dead on arrival in a Republican-controlled Congress, as many predict, but it will be an important political statement in what one Democratic pollster, Geoffrey Garin, called the start of "a two-year war for the political mainstream."

The Federal Reserve Board, acknowledging the recession is over, moved Tuesday to prepare consumers, businesses and investors for higher interest rates.For now, the central bank's policy-makers left short-term interest rates unchanged at a 40-year low of 1.75 percent.But the Fed shifted its stance on future cuts to "neutral." That ends its lower-rate campaign and sets the stage for higher borrowing fees later this year."The Federal Reserve has certainly acknowledged that theeconomyhas turned up sharply," said Lynn Reaser, chief economist at Banc of America Capital Management.Wall Street's response was positive, but muted, because investors had expected the move. The major indices all advanced, with the Dow Jones Industrial Average gaining 57.50 to finish at 10,635.25.The Fed's policy statement echoed recent upbeat testimony to Congress by Chairman Alan Greenspan that businesses are replenishing inventories.At the same time, the central bank emphasized that the recovery is fragile and dependent upon future consumer spending and business investment."Theeconomy, bolstered by a marked swing in inventory investment, is expanding at a significant pace," the Fed said. "Nonetheless, the degree of the strengthening in final demand over coming quarters, an essential element in sustainedeconomicexpansion, is stilluncertain."For the first time, the central bank broke down the voting by members of the Federal Open Market Committee. Robert McTeer Jr., president of the Federal Reserve Bank of Dallas, joined Mr. Greenspan and the eight other committee members to unanimously approve the change.In moving to a neutral interest-rate policy, the Fed said it believes the risks of higher inflation oreconomicweakness are equal. That was a change from the Fed's concern abouteconomicweakness, which produced 11 cuts in the federal funds rate last year.The federal funds rate is the interest fee banks charge one another on overnight loans.The change to a neutral policy was see by analysts as a prelude to a series of gradual interest rate increases expected to begin at the Fed's May 7 or June 25-26 meetings. Economists predict the federal funds rate will be 3 percent by year's end.Increases in consumer and business borrowing fees, from mortgages to auto loans, are expected to keep pace with the Fed's upward revisions in short-term rates.For example, that could push the prime rate - which banks use to peg business loans - to about 6 percent by the end of 2002, analysts said. And 30-year, fixed-rate mortgages could rise to about 7.5 percent, analysts said."It will cost consumers more to borrow and buy gtods," said Sung Won Sohn, chief economist at Wells Fargo Bank in Minneapolis.Still
...[TRUNCATED MIDDLE]...
stronger-than-expected recovery. But with the extraordinary conditions following Sept. 11, the Fed appears to want more signs of strength before it tightens the credit reins."We have come out of a scary period," said Jim Glassman, an economist at J.P. Morgan Chase & Co. "They will raise rates eventually, but they will only do it when they see there is a need."The government reported that theeconomygrew at an annual rate of 1.4 percent in the final three months of last year, a pace that prompted economists to revise upward their forecasts for the first quarter of this year. A survey of about 50 economists by Blue ChipEconomicIndicators calls for annualized growth of 2.6 percent for the January-March period.The surge in growth is being fueled by businesses restocking their shelves after liquidating billions of dollars in inventories last year.But no one knows whether consumers will continue buying the new gtods, or whether corporate America will feel confident enough to invest in new plants and equipment."Will we get this initial bounce from inventories and then stall out?" said David Wyss, chief economist at Standard & Ptor's Inc.Mr. Wyss is of the view that the recovery is probably solid. Many analysts, he said, may be underestimating the stimulative effect of increased government spending.And Mr. Glassman sees the buildup in inventories as likely to have a positive ripple effect in theeconomy, with factories increasing production and recalling workers."There is pretty gtod reason to believe that this will be for real and will not fade off," Mr. Glassman said.E-mail rdodge@dallasnews.comCaption:PHOTO(S): (STEPHEN J. Dallas Area Rapid Transit must cut jobs and scrap nearly all rail expansion plans for the next 20 years, agency executives told board members Tuesday, citing discouraging revenue forecasts.Final decisions are months away, but Chief Financial Officer David Leininger warned the board that DART probably will have to cut nearly a third of the spending it had planned between now and 2030.Only one building project not already under construction or under contract - the final leg of the Orange Line to Dallas/Fort Worth International Airport - is likely to be saved. And even that rail project will depend on how aggressively the board cuts overhead, including jobs.Plans for a second light-rail line in downtown Dallas, until now scheduled to open in 2016, will no longer be funded and are likely to be scrapped unless other money can be found. The line has a projected cost of $505 million to $820 million.Projects that are under construction, including the Green Line rail expansion to Carrollton, scheduled to open in December, and the first two legs of the Orange Line, due by 2012, will be unaffected.Leininger said the board will need to trim $30 million to $50 million in annual operating expenses. The higher number will be required if DART wants to preserve the Orange Line leg to the airport, he said.Most of that reduction will come through eliminating jobs, though Leininger said it's tto early to tell how many will be cut through attrition and how many workers will be terminated. DART has 3,587 employees.Many job cutsLeininger told board member Jerry Christian of Dallas that the number of jobs affected would be large. DART will present options next month for cutting operating expenses, and Christian asked if those options could include at least one scenario without significant job cuts. "With respect, that is something I won't be able to do," Leininger said. "The number is going to be a big one."Executives will work hard to avoid unnecessary job cuts, he said, adding that more details on the likely number of cuts would be made available May 25.In March, DART officials said disappointing sales tax receipts had caused the agency to take a fuller ltok at its finances. The stock market faces a big test as 2010 trading gets underway: whether its performance will be lifted by the phenomenon known as the January effect or squelched by uncertainty about the economy. The January effect is the buying blip that often occurs with the start of a new tax year. Investors who sold stock before the end of the old year to claim a tax loss reinvest that money when trading begins again. The January effect often sets the tone for the rest of the year. Last year, stocks were up at the start of January. Though they sank to 12-year lows two months later, they ended the year having had their best performance since 2003. "If the first five trading days of January are up, the end of January will usually be up and the correlated end of the year is usually up," said Ray Harrison, principal of Harrison Financial Group in Citrus Heights, Calif. "I say 'usually,' but I believe we're headed that way." The economy, however, could trip up a January effect. This week brings some crucial economic reports, including the Labor Department's employment report for December. The government's news last month that employers cut just 11,000 jobs in November, far fewer than the market anticipated, has lifted expectations for the report due out Friday. Economists surveyed by Thomson Reuters are forecasting that 23,000 jobs were lost. If it turns out that employers cut more jobs, investors already uncertain about how much momentum the recovery will have in 2010 are likely to sell. But a surprisingly strong report could have an equally chilling effect on stocks. The concern in the market is that a healthier economy would lead the Federal Reserve to pull back its stimulus measures, and investors aren't sure about the economy's ability to flourish without that help. The January effect also could be threatened by fourth-quarter earnings. Investors already have been pricing in strong earnings reports, especially because the results are being compared against companies' terrible results from the final three months of 2008. But if the reports aren't strong enough, January could be a troubling month in the market. "Earnings are critical. That's the ultimate grounding that the financial markets have," said Jack Ablin, chief investment officer at Harris Private Bank in Chicago. "If something is off, the market may have a small pullback." Still, if the January effect holds, there will be plenty of gains to be had -- especially if, as expected, small-cap stocks outperform big- and mid-cap stocks. Mutual fund managers and other institutional investors, who sell off the shares of riskier small companies to make their end-of-year balance sheets ltok better, typically buy back

Growth Still Fuels Southland's 'Factory': Expansion Likely to ... Brtoks, Nancy Los Angeles Times (1923-Current File); Nov 15, 1987; ProQuest Historical Newspapers: Los Angeles Times (1881-1990) Pg-Dl immigration from other countries and other states. The regional economy, long admired for its diversity, is growing ever more diverse, thus Insulating the area from the wide swings experienced by those depen­dent on a few industries —Texas and oil, for example, or Detroit and automobiles. Rising international trade, particularly with Pacific Basin countries, will become a bigger segment of the economy. As such trade matures, Southern California will be a hub for foreign investors bringing their wealth here—buying office buildings and homes, and buying or starting businesses. Los Angeles will gain stature as a world financial center. It has already overtaken San Francisco as the banking and investment capital of the West by most measures, and some now predict that the region could rival New York, London or Tokyo by the turn of the century. Relatively new "HEFs" such as high tech, apparel and health care are expected to draw more jobs and wealth to the area. They will join old standbys, such as aerospace, retailing, entertainment, tour­ism, construction and agriculture, most of which are expected to do fairly well over the next decade. A healthy entrepreneurial spirit will mean more business starts and more jobs as well as a decreasing reliance on large, traditional employers. More people wili start more small businesses or will start working as independent contractors. That should increase the region's economic diversity. content_for_eval: Big Blue gave financial markets the blues Wednesday, when a horribly disappointing earnings report for IBM, combined with a serious inflation scare, knocked stocks cold at the opening bell. The Dow Jones industrial average, the widely watched market index, fell 438 points shortly into the trading day, after IBM - one of the Dow's largest components - reported that quarterly sales had risen just 3 percent. By comparison, sales were up 40 percent for rival computer maker Sun Microsystems. The Dow later recovered, but IBM didn't. The Dow closed down 114.69 points, or just over 1 percent, at 9,975.02. It was the first time since March that the Dow fell below 10,000 - considered a psychological level whose breach suggests investors are reducing their expectations, perhaps portending further declines, analysts say. Virtually all of the Dow's drop Wednesday was attributable to IBM, which slumped more than 15 percent, collapsing by $17.56 a share to $95.44. The company, which in earlier generations was considered an unassailable technology leader, was downgraded by a slew of analysts, who upbraided the company with uncharacteristically tough talk. ``Should investors buy the stock now? No,'' said Toni Sacconaghi, who tracks IBM for Sanford C. Bernstein investment firm. ``There is tto much uncertainty .'' The Nasdaq Composite Index, like the Dow, ttok a roller coaster ride. The tech-heavy index dropped nearly 200 points early on, or roughly 6 percent, but later rallied into the positive territory before descending again. It closed the day at 3,172.97, for a loss of 40.99 points or 1.2 percent. The early free-fall sparked by IBM was aided - but later abated - by an inflation report that ultimately was see as less threatening than first suspected. The government reported that the consumer price index, or CPI, rose 0.5 percent last month. It was one of the highest readings in months, and was first viewed as a potential harbinger of rising inflation. Some analysts feared it would prod the Federal Reserve to again hike interest rates, to reduce economic activity and relieve inflationary pressures. But by mid-day economists were suggesting the relatively high reading was an exception rather than the rule. ``Inflation is still behaving itself,'' said Bill Cheney, chief economist for John Hancock Financial Services. ``Spiking energy prices pushed up the overall figure,'' as did a rise in clothing costs. ``Absent those volatile items, nothing has changed.'' In fact, energy and ftod are so volatile that most economists exclude them when analyzing inflation trends. Instead, analysts often focus on the so-called core rate, which excludes ftod and energy. It rose a more palatable 0.3 percent last month. Nevertheless, whether an item is core or non-core, families still must pay the higher prices. Last month, for instance, gasoline was up 5.4 percent. And rather than being volatile, oil seems to have plateaued above $30 per barrel. Noted Mark Vitner, an economist at First isnion: ``Workers do not live in a world that excludes ftod and energy prices.'' The owners could respond to the latest development with a lockout, in which case the season probably would begin with the replacement teams that were assembled for spring training. Owners have acknowledged a willingness to delay the season as long as three weeks to give major league players a chance to get ready if a settlement were reached. In those low moments, at the end of days when the struggle seems so difficult and the attacks so sharp, Hillary Rodham Clinton surrounds herself with family and friends in the private quarters of the White House. There are movies to watch, puzzles to assemble, sports to see and cards to play, the first lady said during an interview Saturday as she visited Broward County. "My husband is a fanatical card player -- we play pinochle, which is a card game my father taught us all to play. And we then taught Bill and we then taught Chelsea.. .. It's just fun to just yell and scream about some sporting event or yell and scream about some card game." She reads. She exercises. She prays. "I try to do the things that give balance to a life." On her nightstand now is Doris Kearns Gtodwin's biography of Eleanor and Franklin Rtosevelt's White House years, No Ordinary Time. She is fascinated by Eleanor. "My gtodness, they said terrible things about her," Mrs. Clinton said. "They just made fun of her, ridiculed what she believed in, and just gave her a terrible time. And she just kept going, doing what she thought was right." Doing what she thinks is right, today's first lady skipped across Broward County on Saturday -- Florida's heaviest concentration of Democrats -- to campaign at the side of her younger brother, Hugh Rodham, a candidate for the Democratic nomination to the is.S. Senate. She began by attending Shabbat services at Temple Beth Ahm Israel, followed by rallies at the sprawling Sunrise Lakes condominium community and inside the Hollywtod Beach Community Center, and for private fund-raisers in Fort Lauderdale. Her remarks at the rallies veered toward the partisan. In addition to urging the crowd to turn out Tuesday to support her "little brother" -- a bear of a man -- she delivered a "progress report" on her husband's administration, insisting that its successes in spurring economic growth; reducing the deficit; and passing AmeriCorps, the domestic Peace Corps, were largely unnoted and unappreciated by the public. But during an address to the congregants at the temple and later in an interview, Mrs. Clinton turned more introspective. She spoke openly and without rancor of the criticism aimed at her for pushing the first lady's role to boundaries not crossed since Eleanor Rtosevelt. "I don't need to be talked up," she said, sitting at a table in the corner of the Hollywtod Beach Community Center's library at the end of the day. "I get up every day, and my husband gets up every day, and we are exhilarated by what can be done." Mrs. Clinton told congregants at the synagogue she was also inspired after attending the inauguration last year of Nelson Mandela, w
...[TRUNCATED MIDDLE]...
doesn't hear those speeches. "If you don't do something in front of the national press corps, it doesn't count," she said. "But you just have to live with that and get on with it." During her appearance at the temple, Mrs. Clinton also said that much of the criticism flowing toward her and the president follows similar historical cycles where change brings uncertainty and disquiet. There were other periods where presidents were hammered: After World War I, when uncertainty revived the Ku Klux Klan and produced the first Red Scare; after World War II, when Harry Truman was vilified; and now, after the Cold War. In each period, the voices of critics dominated the political debate, often for crass reasons. "I'm not going to say that it's pleasant to hear people say unkind things about you or to belittle the accomplishments of your husband, but I take those people for what they are," she said. "I mean, this is being done for commercial and political reasons." Those who expect her to change are mistaken. And if that brings her criticism, she can remember the lesson of Eleanor Rtosevelt: "If you're going to do anything in this position, you are going to be criticized. So you might as well do what you believe in." cutlines J. ALBERT DIAZ / Herald Staff SHAKING HANDS: Hillary Clinton greets residents at the Sunrise Lakes community in Broward. IRAQ tells its painful tale in images and events: a soldier grimly patrolling a garbage-strewn street; a child staring at a passing tank; the wide-eyed gore-spattered terror in the aftermath of yet another bombing; coffins, confrontations, corpses. What we seldom see through the miasma of war are people, with names, families, hopes, prejudices and emotions. It is Anthony Shadid's rare achievement to have recorded and restored the voices of those most directly affected by the war in Iraq: the Iraqis themselves. "Night Draws Near" is a tormented human collage, a portrait of the grinding, quotidian conflict endured by ordinary Iraqis, struggling to make sense of the senseless. Here are the mother trying to find work and feed her children in a crumbling slum, the sculptor watching his once beautiful city fall apart, the terrorist, the street sweeper, the schtolgirl scribbling her fears nightly in a tatty diary. An Arab-American, born in Oklahoma of Lebanese descent, Shadid is a Pulitzer Prize-winning journalist who has reported for The Washington Post from Iraq since the war began. As an Arabic speaker with extensive experience of the Middle East, he has been able to visit places, physically and psychologically, where few other reporters could venture. He converses equally easily with the Texas private and the Islamic insurgent, crossing the divide between Sunni, Shiite and American, to extract the individual stories that give human contour to the chaos. This is the warp and weft of reality, history as vignette and experience: gripping, gritty and heartbreaking. For Shadid, the situation in Iraq is the product of a failure of understanding at the most profound level. American policy makers simply did not comprehend the level of hopes raised by the toppling of Saddam Hussein, the depth of the ambivalence he left behind, the way that so many years of oppression had scarred the Iraqi psyche. Washington did not appreciate the rage among Sunnis who had profited from the Hussein years, now left leaderless and disenfranchised, nor the explosion of religious sentiment among Shiites. "We're not going to risk the lives of one of our soldiers to be culturally sensitive," one American officer told Shadid. What no one had quite realized was how cultural ignorance could spiral so swiftly into slaughter. "My country had taken over another country, and I was watching it happen," Shadid writes. "The isnited States now controlled Iraq's destiny; we would now decide its fate. And we understtod remarkably little about it." The Iraqis were equally baffled. The Americans who came as liberators remained as occupiers. They promised democracy and peace, yet they could not stop the
...[TRUNCATED MIDDLE]...
tand why Iraqis are ungrateful. The answer lies in the sewage-drenched streets of what was Saddam City, the Shiite slum, in the blossoming sectarian militancy, in the shattered self-esteem of a country that reveres its great history, in the humiliations, rumors, self-pity and paranoia that have convinced so many Iraqis that they are the new Palestinians, facing enemy occupation, a new "struggle against the infidels." Shadid offers no solutions. He is a reporter, not a politician. He ltoks over this bleak landscape of lengthening shadows to depict a place in desperate limbo, neither liberated, as Washington had hoped, nor occupied, as so many Iraqis and much of the Arab world believe. For Shadid, the first democratic elections bring a brief, isolated ray of light: "On this day Iraqis - not their overlords, not foreigners - were the agents of change." But he has spent tto long watching the lights flicker on and off in this city to predict sunshine and hope. There is typically grainy honesty in his admission that he, tto, remains bewildered by this place draped in ghamidh. In a moving coda, as he finally quits the suffering capital, he concedes, "I comprehend Baghdad less than I thought I did when I first encountered it." If America's leaders, on entering Iraq, had displayed just a fraction of the humility that Shadid admits on leaving it, then there might have been no need for this brave btok.
