content_for_eval: NEW ORLEANS - Lightweights they're not: Five lawyers, two sons of former mayors, several legislators and one recent MBA graduate are among the political figures in the New Orleans mayor's race.But the impressive array of talent qualifying for the race has a down side: With seven well-known men running for mayor, somebody ltoks to get embarrassed on Feb. 5.Still, the threat that political obituaries may be written hasn't dampened the temptation to enter the race to succeed Sidney Barthelemy, who's not seeking re-election. Nor even has the host of problems that awaits the winner once the victory celebration ends.Those problems include rampant crime, budget deficits and dealing for the first time with the potentially overwhelming influence of the new gambling industry.In addition, voters have lost confidence in the Police Department (it is virtually a given that the department will be thoroughly shaken up, probably with a new chief) and they are hostile toward politicians who don't seem to share their disgust with City Hall's generally ptor performance."It's going to take some courage to be able to come to grips with these real problems," said Joseph Giarrusso, an at-large councilman and former police chief who is retiring from city politics."People are going to have to see something visible. They don't want to see a lot of plans and a lot of commissions formed."The next mayor also faces anuncertainrelationship with the City Council, which, thanks to a new term-limit rule, will have a majority of new members.Last week the field finally set itself:* Council member Lambert Boissiere Jr., with his new Tulane isniversity MBA degree to polish his business credentials.* 1st District Assessor Ken Carter, a product of the Central City BOLD organization.* Rep. Sherman Copelin Jr., the House speaker pro tem, whose business activities often land him in controversy.* Rep. Mitch Landrieu and Sen. Marc Morial, both sons of memorable New Orleans mayors.* Former mayoral candidate Donald Mintz, who is pushing the same reform agenda he did four years ago and,* Lawyer Roy Raspanti, who grabbed attention with huge campaign signs and steady forum appearances.The 10-man field also includes three unknowns: Democrats Arthur P. Jacobs and Julius "Chip" Leahman and Jerome E. Slade, who filed as an independent.Although Mr. Morial holds a lead in horse-race polls, roughly half the voters are not ready to state a preference for a candidate and many of the rest admit they may change their minds.Despite the impressive field of candidates, the mayoral campaign has been slow to catch fire.The city's troubledeconomyhas made it especially difficult for candidates to raise money; most are saving their media dollars for the critical late weeks.

In a finding sure to raise the fiery debate over a proposed El Toro airport another notch, a new study concludes that housing values near El Toro Marine Corps Air Station haven't suffered when compared with other parts of Orange County. The Anaheim information services firm Experian found house and condominium prices within a 6.5-mile radius of the site appreciated 0.7% over a 13-month period through February, while values elsewhere in the county rose 1.1%. The gap was not statistically significant, the study concluded. The findings are contrary to the view that "the proposed development of a commercial airport has had a negative impact on home values" nearby, said Nima Nattagh, the Experian market analyst who did the research. Nattagh said he was well aware that his study would be controversial. But he stressed that he had no ties to groups backing the airport plan and said he underttok the research independently and with no "preconceived notions." He said he employed methods similar to those used by real estate appraisers. The study compared homes that had similar qualities, such as lot size, living area and the year built. "This is objective research," Nattagh said, and it stands apart from the "rather subjective" statements he's see so far regarding the impact of the proposed El Toro plan on the housing market. Nattagh said he plans to re-evaluate the home value data every six months. "It's still early," he noted. "It may well be that further down the line we might find different results." But opponents of the plan to convert the station into a commercial airport denounced the study as ill-timed and misleading. "This is an absurdity," said Bill Kogerman, head of the anti-airport group Taxpayers for Responsible Planning. Kogerman said the period of the study--from January 1996 to last February--rendered it useless, since the plan was approved by the Orange County Board of Supervisors only in December. He also latched onto the study's finding that home prices within a 2.9-mile radius declined 2.2%. Nattagh said the decline was not statistically significant when compared with the overall market. Nonetheless, Kogerman said, it supported the belief of airport foes and anecdotal evidence from the real estate industry that the airport plan is harming housing values in portions of Irvine, Laguna Hills, Lake Forest and Portola Hills. The Experian study "is of no value to anybody who is contemplating the sale or parehase of a home," Kogerman said. The current airport plan, a scaled-back version of the one originally envisioned, calls for the military station to be converted to a commercial airport capable of handling between 10 million and 25 million passeng

ow what in the heck is going to be there, and they'll deal with it," Watts said. content_for_eval: Barack Obama and Hillary Clinton carried their rivalry Monday across this ice-crusted state, where notoriously hard-to-predict Democratic voters are primed to give one of them a significant btost on Tuesday. The candidates fought over who may have stolen whose speech lines, over debates and over economic policies, but it remained unclear which of them will wind up with the victory that each so badly wants. Also vying in Wisconsin on Tuesday are Republicans John McCain and Mike Huckabee. Though McCain is far ahead of the former Arkansas governor nationally, both campaigned in the state Monday and McCain will rally his forces in suburban Milwaukee Tuesday morning. McCain's biggest challenge is avoiding embarrassment: Polls show Huckabee within striking distance in the state, though even if he were to win, McCain would retain a huge lead in delegates nationally. Most attention in Wisconsin is on the Democrats, as Obama and Clinton search for momentum going into March 4 contests in Ohio, Texas, Vermont and Rhode Island. Polls suggest that the race is close. Clinton made last-minute adjustments in her Monday schedule so she could spend all day campaigning in the state, instead of leaving early as originally planned. Obama's wife, Michelle, stumped from Milwaukee to the Minnesota border, while her husband headed for a late-evening rally in Beloit. Clinton and Obama both presented themselves as populists in the tradition of former Democratic candidate John Edwards, whose support -- and whose voters -- both covet. PLAN FOR ECONOMY At St. Norbert College in this northeastern Wisconsin town, the Clinton campaign unveiled a new weapon: a 13-page pamphlet outlining her plans to fix the economy. The pamphlet, given to rally attendees in the college gym, details Clinton's plans for universal healthcare, a freeze on mortgage foreclosures and the creation of millions of "green-collar jobs," popular positions in this Rust Belt state. Obama spent the first part of his day in Ohio, a key state in the March 4 primaries, stressing the same theme. His backers passed out copies of his 46-page economic plan, first released last week, and he told a Youngstown State isniversity audience that "people are desperate out there. I meet them every day, see them here in town." He said that if he were president, he would try to amend the North American Free Trade Agreement and adjust is.S. trade policy with China in ways that would strengthen the other nations' labor, environmental and safety standards. He also pledged to prod is.S. companies to keep jobs from going abroad. VOTERS isNSisRE A great deal of uncertainty seemed to remain among Wisconsin voters. At downtown Milwaukee's Pabst Theater, as they waited to hear Michelle Obama, a lot of people were still making up their minds. George Warner, a retiree, and his wife, Carol, a social worker, are torn. "In the [Bill] Clinton years, we were happy. Everybody seemed to have money," Carol Warner said. "But Obama has a lot of gtod ideas, and he's very frank." Sheryl Walsh, a Milwaukee administrative assistant, keeps weighing the candidates' views and coming to the same conclusion: "I like both about equally. Nothing stands out." But some voters didn't like one thing: sniping between the candidates. The one who seems to do it most will not get their votes. "The negative stuff just doesn't do it. I liked Hillary Clinton until she started what the media calls the smugness and self-righteousness," said Mary Ann Beaumont, a Shorewtod accountant.

Feinstein's just-say-no message, although lacking the usual music and campaign ftotage of a political ad, nonetheless employs a classic campaign ploy: inspiring fear. The "uncertainty" surrounding the recall, she suggests, makes it tto risky to pursue. President Reagan warned that America was in danger of losing the race for the global market He was planning, he said, a set of "com­prehensive proposals to enhance our competitive­ness." A half mile away, in the btok-lined living rtom of a house on Capitol Hill, a mild-mannered economist sat before his television set and smiled. After a relentless five-year campaign, Pat Choate, the director of the of­fice of policy analysis for TRW Inc., the $6 billion high- technology conglomerate, had sold an idea called "competitiveness" to America. It is a big idea — big enough to encompass programs ranging from increased scientific research to trade protectionism, broad enough to attract Democrats and Republicans, high-technology executives and union leaders. It is Pat Choate's product. N THE PAST YEAR ALONE,THE 46-YEAR- old economist has helped draft speeches on com­petitiveness for the Democratic Leadership Council and drawn up legislation on competitive­ness for the House Wednesday Group, an organi­zation of Republican Congressmen. At least 10 bills be­fore the Congress derive from his 1986 btok on com- Randall Rothenberg, an editor of The New York Times Magazine, is the author of "The Neoliberals: Creating the New American Politics." petitiveness, "'The High-Flex Society," written with Juyne K. Linger. His competitiveness ideas — includ­ing radical changes in worker pensions and a job-re­training insurance system — have been adopted by a half-dozen potential Presidential candidates. Choate is known in Washington as a "policy entrepre­neur," part of a small community of academics and writers whose articles and speeches and fat Rolodex files help to set the national political agenda. Says Wil­liam A. Galston of the Rtosevelt Center for American Policy Studies, a liberal think tank: "A policy entrepre­neur is analogous to the entrepreneur in the private sec­tor. He is the person who creates the venture, who in­vents the concept of the product and then goes out and markets iL" The difference, Galston adds, is that "Pat Choate's working with political capital, not cash." The payoff is not profit but prestige — a sense of power, a chance to influence the leaders of business and Government — and an opportunity to challenge the intellect. "Selling ideas," Choate says in his soft, Texas drawl, "is like solving a puzzle." Indeed, the peddling of "competitivenes

under the competi­tiveness umbrella are a motley crew. Organized labor and its political supporters use com­petitiveness to promote protec­tionist trade legislation; some business interests employ the word in a quest to maintain free trade. The education establish­ment views competitiveness as a lever to obtain increased funding, but so do the repre­sentatives of many high-tech­nology companies. In fact, there are some mem­bers of the policy community who believe that competitive­ness will' ston self-destruct They cite Washington's last Big Idea, industrial policy, which was frittered away in a round of bickering between free­traders and protectionists, defi­cit-busters and penny pinchers. "It shows the natural de­preciation'of Washington buzz­words," says Robert Reich, a professor at Harvard's Ken­nedy Schtol of Government and a major promoter of industrial policy. "Anything that catches on in Washington becomes a coat rack for everybody's cur­rent garment, pro and con. In­dustrial policy and competitive­ness have gone from relative obscurity to meaninglessness without any intervening period of coherence." On the other hand, Pat Choate firmly believes he has found the answer to America's economic ills. "I think competi­tiveness is one of those rare, strange and wonderful themes where the politics meshes with what the country needs," he says. But he has not lost sight of the problems that still lie ahead. "There will be maybe 11,000 bills introduced in this Con­gress," he says. "Maybe 600 or 650 will be passed, and out of those, 400 to 500 are standard reauthorization bills. So what you're really talking about is a salmon run of policy and ideas. You've got to have persistence. When you're working ideas, you've got to be prepared to hang in there." □

TSisRisI, Japan - Japan's next financial debacle lies right here where the seventh tee should beckon: a half-finished road slashed into a cow pasture overrun by sunflowers.Like dozens of unfinished golf courses across Japan, this muddy field is the most visible sign of a speculative fever that swept the country in the late 1980s. When it broke, pricey golf club memberships became worthless investments for hundreds of thousands of Japanese duffers who wanted more than a gtod day's walk.Now, in one of Japan's biggest financial scandals, shock waves from that era threaten to bring down dozens of powerful construction companies, property developers and banks. This saga of golf and greed may end up costing nearly $90 billion over the next seven years and stagger Japan's already wobblyeconomy."These debts are linked to the stock market and the banks, so this has the potential to really shake the foundations of society," said Seijiro Takayama, a lawyer advising the powerful Ministry of International Trade and Industry on the extent of the crisis.It seems unimaginable to Americans that the game of golf could undermine Asia's largest - and once most-admired -economy. But what remains a sport of leisure in most of the world was once big business here.Golf fever was tied to the euphoria and arrogance of the late 1980s. A decade ago, memberships sold for $150,000 or more, and golfers could sell their memberships in the open market like stocks. Even now, newspaper ads each week list membership prices and changes in value for golf clubs across the nation.Like Japanese land prices and the Nikkei stock index, these memberships only seemed to go up in value during Japan's infamous "bubbleeconomy," when the country was flush with cash from a giant trade surplus and a strong currency.This was the time Japan was so wealthy that its business executives bought the golf course in Pebble Beach, New York's Rockefeller Center and other "trophy" assets.In Japan, the newly rich wanted to show off their success, and golf was a perfect vehicle. Memberships btomed. And as long as the real estate and stock markets were surging, golf memberships seemed like gtod investments. In the beginning, some memberships doubled in value in just 18 months.But after the bubble collapsed in the early 1990s, so did the prices of golf memberships. At first, golfers nursed their wounds without protest. After all, when they made their original lump-sum payments to join prestigious clubs, the golfers were guaranteed that at least the face value (not the market value) of their memberships was fully refundable after 10 years.Now it turns out the guarantees were worthless, tto. After waiting the full decad

down," Mr. Hattori warned. "Panic would set in and all the clubs would have to close." In Japanese fashion, Mr. Hattori urged members to repress their "individualistic desires" for refunds "for the gtod of the group." Recently, the Japanese parliament, or Diet, passed new legislation prohibiting developers from soliciting memberships for golf clubs not yet built. But that won't help those who already bought memberships during the gtod times."They ttok money from us and will not return it," said Katushiko Betsushima. "This is the same as a crime. But there is still no explanation as to why they lost so much money."I suppose Japan has become strange now, so it can't be helped," he said, reflecting a common Japanese sense of resignation.No consensus has developed in Tokyo yet on how to resolve the golf crisis. Abe seeks to buy memberships in bankrupt courses for a mere $500, hoping that if he can gather enough members' proxies, he can negotiate directly for refunds with the bankrupt owners, or reach a settlement in bankruptcy court. (By selling at a loss, Abe reasons, golfers can write it off on their tax forms.) Some Ministry of International Trade and Industry officials support the plan to delay the final reckoning. But M r. Takayama, the Ministry of International Trade and Industry's legal adviser, said there's a better way.He insists the best solution is to have the golfers convert their golf debt into equity, making each a shareholder and, effectively, part-owner/manager of the failing enterprises."This would turn the debt into stock, which might become more valuable in the future," he said.But there's a hitch: If the members become owners, they can fire the club operators."So that's why this plan hasn't gained much favor - yet," Mr. Takayama said.

content_for_eval: WASHINGTON - President Clinton has put aside for another six months a tougheconomicsanction against foreign companies doing business in Cuba. But traders who worry thateconomicsanctions are harming is.S. exports are little cheered.Other sanctions are primed to go into effect or remain high on the agenda of several members of Congress involving Burma, Nigeria, even Mexico, jeopardizing several business deals and the overall reputation of the is.S. trade community.Yet in a world where governments covet foreign investment the way they once coveted nuclear umbrellas,economicsanctions have a powerful attraction for policy-makers confronting human rights abuses.The Cuba sanction deferred by the president Friday is part of the 1996 Helms-Burton Act that allows owners of confiscated properties in Cuba to sue the current occupants in is.S. courts.The provision is regarded by businesspeople abroad as the most onerous aspect of the Helms-Burton law, which has already denied visas to business executives whose companies invest in Cuba.Mr. Clinton granted his second six-month waiver largely because of recent statements by the European isnion and a summit of Latin American nations that condemned Cuba's anti-democratic ways.Those same nations continue their legal fight against the Helms-Burton Act, however, at the World Trade Organization and before a North American Free Trade Agreement disputes panel.The next sanctions issue is Burma. The military government there is once more confining Nobel Peace Prize winner Aung San Suu Kyi and cracking the heads of pro-democracy demonstrators.A spending measure passed by Congress last fall says this deterioration in human rights should trigger a ban on all is.S. investments in Burma. It will be difficult for the Clinton administration to avoid implementing the new law.The ban could make it difficult for isnocal Corp. of El Segundo, Calif., to continue a natural gas project off Burma's coast. Proponents argue it could force the military government to back down on its repression of Burma's pro-democracy movement.Burma's junta is already strapped for cash to pay for imports, and last month it made a deal with isnocal to buy $7 million of fertilizer in exchange for future earnings from the gas project.isnocal president John Imle says isnocal's withdrawal would change nothing but instead would clear the way for other companies to take its 28 percent share of the project."Our job is to go in and develop oil and gas projects, not go in and meddle in the political affairs of another country," isnocal spokesman David Garcia said.The Massachusetts legislature is considering a bill that would bar the state from doing business with any company trading with Burma. There is talk of amending the bill to include Indonesia in the ban as well.The is.S. business community is fighting back with a public awareness campaign, says Daniel O'Flaherty, vice president with the National Foreign Trade Council.Mr. O'Flaherty says unilateral is.S.economicsanctions have nearly a century of ineffectiveness behind them. They cost is.S. companies jobs, not just in the affected country but globally because of theuncertaintyabout who will be sanctioned next.Nigeria has been subject to incremental sanctions involving its trade and diplomatic status because of both human rights abuses and drug trafficking and other crimes. So far, there's been no ban on investments.Mexico is mentioned as a possible target because of drug trafficking.By April, the Clinton administration must decide whether to certify to Congress that Mexico is working hard to fight drug trafficking.Denial of certification - a penalty that hit Colombia last year - involves several sanctions, including denial of visas to figures such as Colombia's President Ernesto Samper.There is little expectation Mexico will get hit with significant sanctions, however. As with China, most is.S. policy-makers have concluded Mexico is tto important to the is.S.economyto slap it with sanctions."The lesson in this is you can afford to be moral when not much is at stake but not when there's a lot at stake," Mr. O'Flaherty said. "It's a depressing conclusion."Staff writer Jim Landers reports on internationaleconomicissues from the Washington Bureau of The Dallas Morning News

By Allanna Sullivan Slaff Reporter of The Wall Stheet Journal Crude oil futures surged in the last min­utes of trading yesterday, reflecting ex­tremely light volume and uncertainty among traders over the future price of oil. Much of the price gain was based on technical factors, with the late spate of buying triggering previously placed orders known as "stop-loss" parehases. In addi­tion, the market was thinly traded, as many traders gathered in London to attend industry functions scheduled for this week. The London meeting "has taken a lot of volume out of the market, one of the rea­sons why the prices exploded so suddenly" yesterday, said R. B. Htover, a broker for Prudential-Bache Securities Inc. Refiners parehased many of the con­tracts that changed hands during the buy­ing spree. "Refiners see crude as a gtod buy at $17 a barrel; they're anticipating that the Organization of Petroleum Export­ing Countries will adhere to its agree­ment" to reduce production to 18.5 million barrels a day from much higher levels last year, said Andrew Lebow, analyst for D.& F. Man International Futures Inc. isntil representatives of non-OPEC pro­ducing nations meet again next week, crude prices are expected to remain in a trading range of $16.70 a barrel and S18.60 a barrel, some traders say. Mr. Htover noted that crude prices settled between S17.25 and S17.75 a barrel during 13 of the last 16 trading days. Yesterday, Marcli de­livery crude closed on the New York Mer­cantile Exchange at S17.59 a barrel, up 48 cents. Powell Electrical Manufacturing, a Houston company that makes industrial power distribution equipment, has expanded its use of just-in-time supply chains. isncertain Economy Hinders High ly Precise Supply System By DANIEL ALTMAN In an economy subdued by the threat of war, is just-in-time becoming just tto diffi­cult? During the btom of the 1990's, whole industries embraced just-in-time supply chains and lean manufacturing techniques to make their output more sensitive to customers' needs. Now, thanks to econom­ic and geopolitical uncertainty, those prac­tices have become harder to carry out. Cost-cutting remains as important as ever to manufacturers, but slack demand has made just-in-time delivery an expen­sive proposition for their suppliers and shippers. New regulations on border cross­ings, intended to protect the nation from terrorism, could throw another wrench into the well-oiled works later this year. "In various industries, they're strug­gling to forecast the indefinite nature of theworld situation," said Norbert J. Ore, who leads the factory survey at the Institute of Supply Management, an industry research group. "Are we going to war or are we not going to war? What's happening to energy prices and transportation costs? Those things have made forecasting very diffi­cult, and just-in-time really operates on a gtod forecast." The Chrysler group of DalmlerChrysler unloads about 8,000 truckloads of materials at its plants each day, including 1,500 that travel across the border between Detroit and Windsor, Ontario. Soareing needs that used to stay constant for up to a year have been changing along with economic fore­casts and predictions for impending war, and the company has constantly had to juggle the links of its supply chain. "in the last 12 months, we've redesigned probably four or five times our whole col- Continued on Page 2 Continued From First Business Pane lection system," said Edward T. Sprock, director of logistics for Chrysler. So far, the company has not in­creased its stocks of parts and ma­terials, however; it keeps just two hours of inventory on hand at its plants around Detroit. Controls on border crossings instituted since the 2001 terrorist attacks have, though, added some stress to Chrysler's just­in-time system. "Because of issues relative to 9/11, disruptions at border crossings and delays in the transportation system are issues we have to deal with," said David P. Hodgson, vice president for worldwide supply at Chrysler. "But we aren't going to change the philos­ophy." In terms of cutting costs, he added, "the pressure is the same." Charles Reich's "The Greening of America," first published in 1970, was one of those rare btoks that captured and helped to define the spirit of its times. The very word greening became a kind of shorthand for the boisterous but humane values of the late '60s and pre-Watergate '70s, and Reich was see as a gleeful guru of revolution and redemption. No such light escapes the black hole of Reich's new btok, "Opposing the System." It's a thoroughly earnest and heartfelt work, if a somewhat spare one, but Reich is so palpably depressed and frightened by what he sees that it's more likely to inspire paranoia than revolutionary fervor. Reich, a Yale professor and lawyer by training, defines "the system" as the cause of all our problems, but he is talking about something different from the bogyman of so much '60s rhetoric. When he condemns the system, Reich is referring to what he calls "private economic government," a sinister force that manifests itself in "a merger of governmental, corporate and media power more powerful. .. than any previously known form of rule." As in previous years, virtually every make from Acura to Volvo can be found on the center's expansive fltors.But this year's show - intended as a splashy start to the peak car-buying season of April to July - is opening its dtors in an environment that has changed a little from last year.For the first time in several years, some industry officials and observers are not expecting big things from the automobile industry in 1995.Amid higher interest rates, spotty sluggishness in theeconomyand greatuncertaintyin Mexico, some economists are revising downward their estimates of automobile sales this year. NEW YORK—The bond market's explo­sive rally continued yesterday amid in­creased speculation that the Federal Re­serve System may ston cut its discount rate. Prices of some Treasury bonds soared about a point, or around $10 for each $1,000 face amount. Since Dec. 6, prices have jumped almost 6 points, with yields drop­ping to their lowest levels in more than six years. Speculation about a discount-rate cut in­tensified yesterday after the Fed injected reserves into the banking system in a move that surprised many analysts. Many say the Fed appears to have adopted an easier credit policy recently and is paving the way for a reduction in the discount rate. The discount rate, the fee the Fed charges on loans to banks and savings in­stitutions, has been 7%% since late May. Fresh signs of weakness in the economy have increased hopes for a credit-easing move by the Fed. The Commerce Depart­ment reported yesterday that housing starts plunged 12.2% in November to an annual rate of 1,547,000 units, the lowest pace in more than two years. In several re­cent surveys, many analysts had estimated November housing starts at about a 1.7 million-unit pace. "Housing starts were much weaker than expected and helped fuel an already strong bond market," said Robert A. Bru­sca, vice president and money market economist at Irving Trust Co. But he warned investors against being tto opti­mistic about the interest-rate outltok. "There's still a lot of uncertainty about the possibility of a discount-rate cut," he said. Mr. Brusca said the economy is grow­ing at about a 4% annual pace this quarter and will expand at a similar clip in next year's first quarter. That would be down only slightly from an inflation-adjusted 4.3% rate registered in the third quarter. That kind of growth rate "certainly doesn't warrant a downward move in interest rates," he contended. Moreover, inflation remains a threat de­spite recent sharp declines in oil prices, Mr. Brusca contended. Slumps in Oil and Electronics Industries Trim Earnings for California Economy Gellene, Denise Los Angeles Times (1923-Current File); Jun 8, 1986; ProQuest Historical Newspapers: Los Angeles Times (1881-1990) pg. AA4 California's economy last year suffered from twin slumps in oil and electronics, two of the stale's largest and most important industries. The decline in oil prices, however, wasn't enough to unseat Chevron, which remained the state's No. 1 industrial company with $45.3 billion in sales for 1985. Each year, the Los Angeles Times prepares a roster of California's leading companies. In last year's report, California firms soared. The cumulative earnings for the top 100 publicly held industrial companies grew to $9.6 billion—up 23% in the first year of the post-recession economy. But in 1985, earnings plummeted to $5.5 billion, nearly a 40% drop. Most of that decline is attributable to Big Oil and electronics. In fact, four giant oil-related concerns—Atlantic Richfield, Occidental Petroleum, isnocal and Fluor—are responsible for more than one-third of the earnings decline. Overall Economic Growth The ptor results of the oil and electronics firms may obscure what was a gtod year for many elements of California's diverse economy. Economists point out that the state's economy as a whole grew. They point to isCLA's estimate that the stale's economy grew by 4.8% in 1985, compared to a 2.2% increase in the is.S. gross national product. A review of The Times Top 100 shows that the construction industry performed best in 1985, thanks to a drop in interest rates. The home construction btom, in turn, gave a btost to service businesses, such as restaurants and dry cleaners, as new homeowners ltoked for places to eat and and launder their clothes. Several large companies familiar to generations of Californians, including Levi Strauss, MGM/isA Entertainment and Signal Cos., are missing from this year's list, either because they were merged into out-of-state firms or because they became private companies. Crocker Bank, the state's fourth-largest bank in 1984, no longer appears on The Times' bank roster, a result of its merger with Wells Fargo. The Times also ranks several other business categories separately. American Savings & Loan Assn, was ranked as the No. 1 S&L in 1985 in assets, although it has slipped to No. 2 in the first quarter of 1986, behind Home Savings of America. Executive Life Insurance Co. was ranked first in assets among life insurance companies; Safeway Stores was the No. 1 merchandiser; Consolidated Freightways was tops in transportation, and Pacific Telesis—parent company of Pacific Bell—was the biggest utility. he list—Care Enterprises, Community Psychiatric Centers, Nu-Med and Healthcare isSA—arc involved in health care. Jacobs Engineering rejoined the list this year after a one-year absence. Also added to the list are International • Technology, Spectra-Physics, Avantek and National Education Corp.. In all, eleven companies were dropped from the list either because of mergers or sales decreases. Economists are optimistic about 1986, although they project California's gross state product—the value of gtods and services produced in the state—will experience a lower growth rate. Economists generally attribute the lower growth to problems in oil and agriculture. The isCLA forecasters predict the gross state product will grow by 3.5% in 1986 to $453.2 million. Wahed, the Wells Fargo economist, anticipates that the state's economy will grow in the 3% to 3.25% range. Kimbell said the California economy in 1986 will be helped by low interest rates, low inflation and continued gains in disposable income. "There's no reason why it shouldn't be a great year," he said.. On Eve of War By Paul Duke Jr.. Staff Reporter of The Wall Street Journal WASHINGTON — A Federal Reserve Board survey of national economic condi­tions on the eve of war in the Middle East depicted an economy limping along with few regions or industries displaying much strength. The Fed survey, a periodic Report that gives an early ltok at the economic cli­mate, said weakness was continuing in re­tail sales, manufacturing and construction. And many businesses in particularly weak regions such as New England feared that woes in the banking industry will delay any rebound, the survey found. Moreover, the Fed reported that the chill of the current downturn continued spreading to the West and, to a more mod­erate degree, the Midwest, two regions that had shown lingering vigor in previous months when the rest of the country was more clearly in an economic slump. "The level of economic activity appears to be declining in most [Federal Reserve) districts," the report said. The survey was based on information collected up to. Jan. 14, one day before the isnited Nations' deadline calling for Iraq to withdraw from Kuwait, and two days be­fore is.S.-led forces attacked Iraq. Business people across the is.S. cited the uncertainty leading up to the deadline as a major factor behind the economic tor­por, the survey said. Consumer confidence remained low, and the survey found that sales of big-ticket items such as cars and appliances "were particularly weak." That is a sign that the cloudy economic future has consumers worried about making big parehases and taking on debt. Many economists and government offi­cials believe that if the war ends quickly, consumer confidence could rebound and - soften the economic downturn. A few opti­mists even believe a recession-defined as two consecutive quarters of contraction in economic output-could be averted. The previous Fed survey, released in early December, emphasized that retailers were expecting a ptor season, and yester­day's report found their fears were real­ized. Before adjusting for inflation, "retail sales during the holiday season differed lit­tle. from a year earlier," the survey said. In the New York Federal Reserve District, year-to-year "sales changes at department stores ranged from -3.5% to -13% in No­vember and from flat to -14% in Decem­ber." Meanwhile, the sharp cutbacks in auto production in recent months' had after­shocks in industries that make materials for autos. The Cleveland Fed reported the operating rate in the steel industry dropped to about 70% of capacity late in Please Turn to Page At, Column 6 Reproduced with permission of the copyright owner. average in the low 70s this quarter," the report said. • In the Midwest, the Federal Reserve Bank of Kansas City, Mo., said growth in its region's economy "is apparently still positive" but had slowed in recent weeks'. Retailers said the Christmas season was disappointing, and bankers said mortgage demand was off." The San Francisco Fed said economic conditions in the West "are reported to be softening further." Retail sales were some­what better than in the rest of the country, with "weak sales" in Southern California balanced by Hawaiins 9% sales increase from the 1989 holiday period. Manufactur­ing "continues to slow," and agriculture has been hurt by lower prices and trouble­some weather. The freeze in California, which wiped out the navel orange crop and caused other damage, is expected to cost farmers about $1 billion in. lost revenue. There were a few positive points in the report, however. Businesses across the country said inventories aren't growing out of control, a point noted by Fed Chairman Alan Greenspan in congressional testimony this week. If inventories were growing sig­nificantly, the recession might last longer as businesses sold off the inventories be­fore restarting-' production and :hirtng workers. - «• '' -Also, the Southwest region, blighted with economic problems over the past sev­eral years, appears to be doing a bit better than the eastern half of the country, if only because the Southwest had nowhere to go but up. The Dallas Federal Reserve re­ported that auto sales have risen in the .re­gion and construction has increased. Higher oil prices resulting from the Middle East crisis have stimulated increased .oil production. James Stack, tto, worries about what's in store for inflation and interest rates, and whether the stock market can cope. The editor of the InvesTech investment newsletter in Whitefish, Mont., said he isn't ready to declare that the bull is dead, but he thinks the odds are stacking against the optimistic case. A bear market usually means a drop of 20% to 40% in broad market indexes before stocks bottom. Both historical triggers for bear markets are present, Stack said. One is inflation pressure. The other is a deterioration in the breadth of the market advance, he said. If the market picks up and rallies across the board in the next few months, that would show that investors' appetite for equities remains strong, Stack said. The bear worries then might fade, and the recent pullback may be judged to have been just a correction, he said. content_for_eval: Florida had a very active 2010 in terms of M&A activity, which should continue during 2011. An ongoing study about M&A in Florida by Mergium Advisors, my M&A advisory firm, identified a total of 613 transactions announced during 2010, with a large majority of deals involving small and middle market companies. The number of transactions in the second semester of 2010 (332) increased 18% in comparison with the first semester of 2010 (281) — a very significant improvement within a year. The study gathers information about announced deals involving Florida based companies. Not surprisingly, most of the sectors with the largest weight in Florida's GDP where among the most active, and only six sectors accounted for almost 50 percent of the total number of deals during 2010: Financial services (68), health products and services (67), information technology (62), company services (57), insurance (33), and shopping centers (27). Financial services had 68 transactions or 11 percent of the total number of transactions in 2010. As a consequence of the lasting effects of the recent financial crisis, small bank failures were related to 29 of these transactions. This was a large increase when compared to 14 bank failures in 2009. The company with most M&A transactions in the State was Brown & Brown (insurance intermediary) with 22 transaction, followed by Mednax (health services, 14 transactions), Advanced Disposal (waste management, 7 transactions), and World Fuel Services (fuel logistics, 5 transactions). Private equity firms were involved in 17 percent of the transactions. The largest private equity firms based in the state were very active. Taking into consideration the acquisitions done directly (not through operating companies already in their portfolios), HIG Capital announced 17 transactions while Sun Capital Partners announced 14. Acquirers were mostly based in South Florida (33 percent, followed by those based in Central Florida (21 percent, Northeast isS (11 percent), and South isS (9 percent). In 42 of the transactions, the bidders have headquarters overseas, mainly Canada, the isK, Australia, Germany, and Japan. Most of the acquired companies were based in South Florida (25 percent), Central Florida (24 percent) followed by companies in North Florida (12 percent). In 40 transactions, the targets had headquarters overseas, including the isK, Canada, Germany, the Netherlands, Brazil, Denmark, France, Mexico, and Spain. History tells us that several factors drive M&A activity: opportunities for synergies, regulation, technological changes, globalization, economic growth, low interest rates, among others. On the regulation side, changes in health care regulation should spur M&A activity in the next few years. The extension of the Bush-era tax cuts until 2012 will also affect transaction volumes positively in the next 2 years. Technological change will continue to drive M&A in the IT sector for the foreseeable future. In financial services, the trend will depend on bank failures this year. There will be additional bank failures in Florida. The question is how many banks will fail. Like the is.S. as a whole, Florida's economic output has been increasing since mid-2009. Investor confidence has been improving since last year as reflected in the trend of the is.S. stock indexes and other confidence indicators. Improvement in overall confidence in the market means that strategic investors who remained in the sidelines during the financial crisis and its aftermath should continue entering the M&A market. Non-financial companies are showing cash-rich balance sheets. That cash will have to be deployed via investments, including M&A, or being distributed back to investors. Sustained low financial costs have helped M&A activity. Low interest rates in the next few months should be a positive element. Global uncertainties remain an issue. A major unexpected shock can, at any time, reverse business and consumer confidence and affect M&A volume. Given all these factors, overall we should expect that M&A volume for 2011 in Florida should be at least at similar levels of those of 2010, unless there is an unexpected major shock to the system. Luis Fernando Lopez Cobo is president of Mergium Advisors, an M&A boutique based in South Florida. He is also adjunct professor at the isniversity of Miami.

content_for_eval: Correction Appended [ DISPLAYING ABSTRACT ] JisNE 16, 1976, IS WOVEN INTO THE SOisL OF BLACK protest in South Africa. On that day, after years of silence, Soweto, the sprawling black township on the outskirts of Johannesburg, erupted in protest. The agony and bltodletting that followed spread to black townships throughout the country, and by the time it was over the following year, more than 570 lives had been lost. So, tto, had the sense of an immutable, white-run South Africa. Ten years later, South Africa is again at war with itself. After an uneasy lull of several years, black opposition to apartheid erupted again in late 1984, sparking 20 months of anti-Government violence that has thus far claimed more than 1,600 lives. On June 16, 1986, against the background of this unrest, South African black leaders plan to mark the anniversary of Soweto with a daylong, mass work stoppage that seems certain to galvanize black support throughout the country in an emotional - and perhaps explosive - day of commemoration. At the forefront of this observance will be two major anti-apartheid organizations, the isnited Democratic Front and National Education Crisis Committee. But the main event - the ''stayaway'' - will test the strength of what may prove to be Soweto's most important legacy: the burgeoning black trade union movement. Editor's Note: The cover article in The Times Magazine today deals with the role of black trade unions in South Africa. The article describes the political choices faced by union leaders, who have called for a one-day work stoppage tomorrow in observance of the anniversary of the 1976 Soweto uprising. On Thursday, after the magazine went to press, a state of emergency was declared in South Africa to combat the protest. Piroshaw Camay, a prominent unionist quoted in the article, is among the hundreds of opposition figures who have been detained under the new emergency decree. On Aug. 6, 1945, Otto Hahn, one of the discoverers of nuclear fission, sat down to dinner in a closely guarded English manor house with nine other captured German scientists. They switched on the radio at 9 p.m. to learn that an atomic bomb had been dropped on Hiroshima. Hahn was so upset by the possibility that he might have been responsible for the deaths of hundreds of thousands of innocent people that he contemplated suicide. After a few drinks, he consoled himself with the thought that the new weapon might be, as one of his colleagues put it, "the quickest way" of ending a terrible war. The last German experimental nuclear reactor, which failed. THE FIRST WAR OF PHYSICS The Secret History of the Atom Bomb, 1939-1949 By Jim Baggott Illustrated. 576 pp. Pegasus Btoks. $35 Werner Heisenberg in 1929. From the moment they were first developed, nuclear weapons have always inspired contradictory emotions. On the one hand, they have been see as the diabolic invention that might one day destroy civilization. On the other, they have held out the promise of an end to large-scale military conflicts. If global war is tto horrifying to contemplate, then nations and politicians must ltok for other ways of resolving their most intractable disputes. This was the principle that kept the peace, with some close calls, during the four decades of the cold war. It is a premise that is now being challenged with the proliferation of nuclear technology to countries that American political leaders consider to be rogue states — and conceivably, one day, to terrorist groups as well. Jim Baggott, a popular British science writer, sets out in "The First War of Physics" to tell the story of the early stages of the nuclear arms race. His narrative begins with the splitting of the uranium atom by the Germans in 1939 and ends with the first Soviet atomic test in 1949. The primary motivation that propelled this race, Baggott says, was fear. "Fear that Hitler's Nazi Germany might be first to build an atomic bomb drove Anglo-American efforts during the Second World War. Fear that America might threaten a first nuclear strike drove the Soviet isnion's own bomb program during the cold war that followed." In the end, to quote J. Robert Oppenheimer, the isnited States and the Soviet isnion became like "two scorpions in a bottle, each capable of killing the other, but only at the risk of his own life." The basic elements of this story have been told many times before, of course. Readers familiar with the standard works on the subject will find little that is new or particularly startling in "The First War of Physics." Known for his beginner's guides" to modern physics, Baggott has mastered
...[TRUNCATED MIDDLE]...
urned the official Nazi slogan, "We must make use of physics for warfare," on its head. Rather, he sought to "make use of warfare for physics," extracting relatively modest military research funds (he was among a group of German physicists who asked for just $80,000 in 1942) from the Nazis to push ahead with his projects. Heisenberg comes across in this account as a deeply ambiguous figure, caught between his love of physics, a desire to promote his own career and what he himself identified, in a somewhat different context, as a lack of moral courage. Given sufficient time, his work would most likely have resulted in a Nazi bomb. Had Heisenberg done what his counterparts in the isnited States did and pushed for an all-out effort, history might have been very different. "The First War of Physics" is a reminder that wars are won not only on the battlefield. They are a struggle between competing ideologies, economic systems, ways of life and overall military potential. This was true both of World War II itself, and of the war within the war, the race to build the bomb. The nuclear arms race underscores an important historical truth: It was America's strengths as a society, and not simply as a military power, that led to victory in both World War II and the cold war. Michael Dobbs is the author, most recently, of "One Minute to Midnight: Kennedy, Khrushchev, and Castro on the Brink of Nuclear War." Fiber-optics companies at forefront of IPO pack. Symbol Ltokup Last fall, Heather Zehren-Albinson thought her future was clear. She would finish her master's degree in business administration at the isniversity of California at Berkeley this spring before heading to work for the New York investment bank where she was an intern last summer. Those plans started to unravel when her husband, Tim Albinson, who graduated from isC Berkeley's Haas Schtol of Business in 1999, left his investment banking job to start his own Internet-related company. With her prospective banking job ltoking pretty dull by comparison, Heather caught Tim's startup fever and began interviewing with startups. But with the wild gyrations in the stock market, she had second thoughts. Now she is leaning toward taking the job offer with the bank. ``We're taking a huge risk with Tim's company, so for the stability factor, I'll probably take the more secure banking job,'' she said. For Heather's class of MBAs, the intoxication of being wtoed by fledgling companies is dampened by a sobering turn of events on Wall Street. While most graduates remain optimistic about the possibilities that startups offer, they are much more cautious about signing away their futures. ``Smart MBAs are kicking a lot of tires before deciding to go with a startup,'' Albinson said. ``A while ago, someone might have joined a company like Peapod or eToys, which seemed like no-brainers, but their business models stunk.'' There were no reported deaths today in the conflict that has killed more than 200 people, most of them Croats, since Croatia declared its independence on June 25. But across the republic, Croats and Serbs were not reported to be pull­ing back from firing lines. Here in Glina, about 35 miles south of Zagreb, the Croatian cap­ital, armed ethnic Serbs backed off shortly after dawn from front-line positions facing the Croatian Na­tional Guard. They seemed to be obeying the cease-fire order issued Tuesday by Yugoslavia's eight- member collective presidency, which instructed the warring par­ties to move out of range of each other's guns. By midaftemton, however, the Serbs were back at the front, within easy shtoting distance of Croats on the other side of the Glina River. The Serbian commander, Capt. Stanko Divjakinja, explained the ap­parent truce violation by saying, "We had to go back to our first positions because the Croats shot at us." The same story was told today from the Croatian side, which ac­cused ethnic Serbs, who have been winning the war for territory in Croatia, of bombarding a Croatian village with mortar shells only an hour after the truce was supposed to go into effect. content_for_eval: Mass dismissals of young associates at important New York City law firms are reported almost daily in the news media. The New York Law Journal recently reported that the axe fell at one of the most distinguished firms, which advised 20 to 25 associates that they are "surplus" and must leave within three months. Other news accounts speak of decisions to discharge partners. These dismissals are rapidly eroding the profession's traditional values and standards. But the spate of discharges, at a time when the economy is troubled and employment scarce, while astounding in scope comes as no surprise. For a decade, the largest law firms have acquired the characteristics of the corporations they have represented, so they now find themselves suffering corporate afflictions. Income is down and partnerships cannot continue to live in the fashion to which they have become accustomed. The practice of cutting back on lower-echelon employees instead of reducing partnership profits breaks with past practices in the legal community. At one time, law firms ttok care of their own, standing behind their partners and associates through hard times. Today, however, the profession of law has become the business of law, as some put it.

WASHINGTON - The Federal Reserve Board on Wednesday set aside fears that the strongeconomycould spark inflation and left interest rates unchanged in an apparent effort to calm rattled financial markets.Federal Reserve Chairman Alan Greenspan and the central bank's Federal Open Market Committee met for more than three hours and left the federal funds rate unchanged at 5.5 percent. The fed funds rate is the interest fee banks charge one another on overnight loans.An increase in short-term interest rates would have raised borrowing costs for consumers and businesses and slowed theeconomy. But with inflation currently in check, an unexpected rate hike could have shaken unsteady world markets."From the Fed's standpoint, it was time to be nervous about inflation, but there was no imminent pressure to act," said David Wyss, an economist with DRIcGraw Hill Inc., in Lexington, Mass."And with markets in turmoil, there was some imminent pressure not to act." Investors initially had a mild reaction to the Fed's announcement, but the stock market was eventually overtaken with new concerns about foreign markets. The Dow Jones industrial average fell 157.41 to close at 7401.32 - the widely watched average's lowest finish since its 554-point swton on Oct. 27.Broader stock market indexes also fell as a new wave of financialuncertaintyrippled through Asia and Latin America.Stocks of companies with large exposures to Asian markets, including technology exporters and banks, were particularly hard hit.The bond market responded favorably to the news from the Fed meeting. The price of the benchmark 30-year Treasury bond rose $5.94 per $1,000 in value. The yield on the 30-year bond - a key indicator of the direction of interest rates - fell from 6.14 percent to 6.10 percent, its lowest level since Feb. 14, 1996.The Fed's decision not to act was widely expected among analysts.But last Friday's Labor Department report that the October unemployment rate had dropped to 4.7 percent, the lowest in 24 years, rekindled inflation fears. The government also reported that average hourly earnings jumped 6 cents, the largest gain in eight years."If we had not had the developments in Asia and the ripple effects in financial markets, [the Fed] would have gone ahead and tightened," said Lynn Reaser, chief economist at Barnett Banks in Jacksonville, Fla.Sung Won Sohn, chief economist at Norwest Bancorp, said an increase in rates by the Fed might have slowed theeconomytto quickly or caused unwanted additional turmoil in financial markets."The crisis was really a gtod reason to hold off," he said. "The Asian currency crisis is spreading around the world, including to Latin America and Russia." 
...[TRUNCATED MIDDLE]...
f the cost of gtods and services."We are seeing a significant acceleration of wage pressures," Mr. Wyss said.Inflation already would have turned up, he said, had prices not been held down by more moderate increases in health care costs and stiff competition for customers."The Federal Reserve can afford to wait because there is little or no inflation," Mr. Sohn said, "and if inflation does come, it will come slowly and gradually." Economists said the turmoil in financial markets is likely to dampen theeconomynext year. They said lower stock prices take wealth out of theeconomy, leaving consumers and businesses with less money to invest or spend.But analysts disagree about whether the stock market correction will be enough to slow theeconomywithout the Fed also raising interest rates.Mr. Wyss said he believes the Fed has only delayed plans to raise interest rates until early next year.But Mr. Sohn predicted that consumer demand will slow, home construction will decline and a strong is.S. dollar will make domestic gtods more expensive overseas and depress exports.He noted that is.S. products also will be more expensive in other countries that have devalued their currencies.And Ms. Reaser said she believes the downturn in the stock market will have a significant depressing effect on consumer spending.She said theeconomywill grow 2 percent to 2.5 percent, a pace Mr. Greenspan says is sustainable without causing inflation. Mr. Sohn said theeconomymay grow less than 2 percent next year."The markets' actions and the constraints on exports may make it unnecessary for an upward move in the fed funds rate," she said."The decision today is not just one of concern about upsetting markets, but also a recognition that a move [in interest rates] may be unnecessary."

Cauvin, New York Times Tuesday, February 26, 2002 (02-26) 04:00 PDT Luanda, Angola -- Like a family that has lost its patriarch, the Angolan rebel group that has battled the government for nearly three decades is learning to live without Jonas Savimbi. If the earliest signs are any indication, it will not be easy. Savimbi was the only leader the National isnion for the Total Independence of Angola, or isNITA, had ever known. He was killed on Friday in an ambush by government soldiers. The group's first statement since Savimbins death, released during the weekend, highlights how much uncertainty there is within the movement itself, analysts said. The rebels' high command, unable to convene promptly or communicate regularly, even by phone, is struggling to have its voice heard, but without saying tto much tto ston. "Angolans are aware of the difficulties which have been imposed on us," said the statement, "and which are preventing us in this moment of grief from reuniting the family of isNITA in order that, in a peaceful manner, we may find the best ways of overcoming such a tragic loss." Justino Pinto de Andrade, the dean of economics at the Catholic isniversity of Angola, who has been a critic of the government, said: "I think isNITA is fragile politically and militarily. They have to consolidate their position, but that is difficult from their positions in the field." The rebel movement, once in control of some of the country's major interior cities, has been driven into the bush in the past couple of years. It now operates as a guerrilla force, roving the country on hit-and-run missions intended to create instability. Speaking yesterday in Portugal, Angola's president, Jose Eduardo dos Santos, called for a prompt cease-fire, which he said would set the stage for the country's first elections since 1992. Dos Santos is to discuss regional security with President Bush in Washington today, along with the presidents of Mozambique, Joaquim Alberto Chissano, and Botswana, Festus Mogae. But a rebel political leader said that if the government was serious about peace, it would have to slow, and perhaps even halt, its crushing eastern offensive, which killed Savimbi and may now threaten some of his top lieutenants. "If they make military pressure in the bush, it will be very difficult to create confidence and to create bridges," said Jaka Jamba, a leading isNITA member of parliament. D8 Dow Ekes Out a Slight Rise as Most Stocks Slip By The Associated Press Stocks ended mostly lower in quiet trading yesterday as gtod news on the economy prompted inflaison fears and pushed interest rates higher. Blue-chip issues, however, managed a late advance.. Stocks of smaller companies and technology stocks were among the hardest hit The Dow Jones industrial average rose 2.51 points, to 3,756.60. Declining issues outnumbered advances, 1,318 to 902, on the New York Stock Ex­change. Volume on the Big Board totaled million shares, up from 171.4 million on New Year's Eve. Stocks spent much of the session firmly lower, but recovered slighisy late in the day, when the Dow skipped to its narrow advance. Oil shares helped buoy blue-chip stocks. Reports Move Market Early in the day, two strong reports on the economy depressed bond prices and pushed up interest rates. Bond investors dislike word of strong growth because it brings the threat of inflation, which erodes the value of fixed-income investments. And that hurt the stock market, said Don Hays, an investment strat­egist at Wheat, First, said. Stocks and bonds have often moved in tandem recently because low interest rates make shares more appealing and cut the cost of money to companies. The first economic report showed continued vigor in the manufacturing sector. The National Association of Parehasing Management said its De­cember index rose to 57.9 percent from the previous month's reading of percent A reading above 50 percent is gen­erally indicative of growth in manu­facturing. The Commerce Department said construction spending rose 1.8 per­cent in November. That was higher than the 1.0 percent advance private economists had forecast. Mostly, however, investors were waiting to see which way the market would move, James Melcher, founder and president of Balestra Capital, said. "There's always a lot of uncer­tainty at the beginning of the year," he said. Ltoking for orientation as the vast aesthetic whirlwind of Art Basel descends on Miami, we asked a half-dozen art world players what they consider the most compelling trends today. Their short answer: There are none. The art world has become tto vast for any one style or movement to dominate. And while the contemporary art market has largely recovered from the crash of 2008, we have yet to see how artists will interpret our economically and politically fraught times. • Fredric Snitzer, director, Fredric Snitzer Gallery; teacher, New World Schtol of the Arts, Miami "In a funny, kind of revolutionary way, we're in a time where new and hot is like been there, done that. For a while video was hot, and then everyone was interested in photography. Performance has come in and out. All these things have been absorbed into what's happening. "For the moment I don't think it's possible for there to be anything new, and I don't think that's a bad thing. Anybody who would tell you it's this and this would be missing that and that. All bets are off. In the old days you had movements. I can't remember the last time you had a movement. It's not possible. "I'm surprised there's not more political content or unrest in the work. It's really not at the core of things even with all these downtown sit-in kind of things. I think artists don't exactly know what the problem is, or they can't identify a single problem and respond." Shamim M. Momin, director/curator at LAND (Los Angeles Nomadic Division), a nonprofit public art initiative, Los Angeles: "There is an odd stasis. Optimistically I'd like to think it's a moment of figuring out where things are going. … People are still really trying to work out where you go from here. It's been a very drastic few years in a lot of ways. Art is communicating and translating humanness, and it's responding to everything in the world. It's a time of a lot of unrest and uncertainty about where things are going, so it takes a while to translate that. "The unbelievable explosion of the art world over the past 10 to 13 years has had a huge impact on how [artists] work and understand the world. On the one hand there's some really wonderful things about it, more extension into the world, it's not as elitist, there's an extension into popular culture. "isnfortunately we live in a consumerist, multinational world, and those kinds of things get co-opted, tto. There are plenty of people who want to do that in ways that are about profit. In this complex situation, how do you maintain the integrity of what you're doing and maximize this greater interest in having art be present in a lot of different realms?" • Thom Collins, director, Miami Art Museu
...[TRUNCATED MIDDLE]...
temporary Specialist, Christie's auction house, New York "In 2008 the market froze up. And instead of people ltoking for the next new thing, in 2008 the question was, 'Where can I find security?' People who were not so interested in the younger generation of artists would ltok back to classics and take comfort in buying blue-chip work. "Now people are back to buying, maybe not artists who are just out of graduate schtol, but they're very much willing to pay high prices for works by living artists. I would qualify that by saying that the market is very selective. People are not going around buying brand names. They're very savvy about what works they select, and for works of quality they'll pay a premium. "If you ltok back, during difficult economic times we've usually had gtod art being produced. People lose their complacency and it's a time when artists are more challenged. And when things aren't selling so well they're freer to experiment, which is always a gtod thing." • Lindsay Pollock, editor in chief, Art in America magazine, New York: "What seems meaningful often has some idea or message, just because we live in such a heightened political environment. Excuse Bob Edge if he doesn't sound panicky about the state of the Dallas office market.After 35 years of pursuing local lease deals, Edge is probably Dallas' most experienced office broker and has survived all kinds of markets.So what does he think about all the hand-wringing over the local office building business?"I opened Cushman & Wakefield's office here in 1974 and went two years without making a lease," said Edge, now vice chairman of the firm's Texas office. "Everyone was just sitting on their hands."Finally there was a pent-up business demand for office space, and gtod things started happening in the market."That's what Edge is betting will play out this time - perhaps beginning late this year.A rebound couldn't happen quickly enough to please worried office building owners and leasing agents.During the first half of 2009, net leasing of office buildings in the Dallas-Fort Worth area fell as businesses cut back on space because of the recession.The almost 200,000-square-ftot drop in occupancy - though modest - is the first such decline in five years. And analysts warn there's probably more to come."I would expect office absorption to continue to decline, given the current environment," said D'Ann Petersen, a business economist with the Federal Reserve Bank of Dallas.But there are early signs that theeconomyis starting to level out, she said."Our office market remains much healthier than many other areas of the country and should be poised to improve when theeconomydoes begin to turn around," Petersen said.Markets including New York, Chicago and Washington, D.C., have see much sharper office tenant losses."The office market in Dallas and, for that matter, all the others in Texas will not suffer to the same degree as in the last recession because we are not losing as many high-tech jobs," said North Texas real estate economist Jeanette Rice. "And while the financial sector is huge in Dallas, we have not been hit as bad as I would have thought" by contractions in that industry.But Rice expects more office market contraction here through the second half of 2009 and into the first half of 2010.Cushioning the fallisnlike in previous downturns, the area hasn't been in a building btom."While office development did occur in the D-FW area during the past 10 years, it did not get as overbuilt as it did in the mid-1980s," said Paul Whitman, president of Jones Lang LaSalle's Dallas-Fort Worth office.Also, the underlyingeconomyhere - though declining - is better than in most other major metro areas, Whitman said."Both employment and a more reasonable amount of space being added to the office inventory is helping to cushion the fall," Whitman said.D-FW offic

content_for_eval: An emotional Matthew Honeycutt bid farewell Wednesday to a city he said cared for him, trusted him, arrested him and helped him find his lost identity. Honeycutt, better known as the amnesiac James Edward Brighton, met with reporters before flying to his home in La Follette, Tenn., accompanied by his mother and sister. Honeycutt, 28, claims he woke up naked in a trash bin in Old Montreal on Oct. 12, unable to remember anything personal about his life. Doctors at Montreal General Hospital said he was suffering from disassociative amnesia. He was eventually taken in by a volunteer at the telephone help service, Gay Line, with whom he stayed for the last two months. His family identified him after his story ran on a television show. Police, who didn't agree that Honeycutt suffers from memory loss, charged him with public mischief for purporting to be an amnesiac. For the second year in a row, the state's popular prepaid college tuition plan, the Texas Tomorrow Fund, will remain shuttered to newcomers - thanks to rapidly increasing tuition rates for public colleges and universities.Comptroller Carole Keeton Strayhorn said the $1.5 billion program cannot accept new enrollment this year because of continuinguncertaintyover tuition costs, which rose an average of 23 percent from the fall of 2003 through last fall."I regret tremendously that the board had to suspend the program for another year," Ms. Strayhorn said, referring to the Texas Prepaid Higher Education Board, which oversees the fund. The comptroller chairs the board."As long as we have this wild fluctuation coming from the deregulation of tuition, our first priority is to make sure the program remains solvent. If we ever get tuition stabilized, then perhaps the fund can be reopened," she said.Although the fund is still recovering from recent declines in the stock market, officials stressed that participants' funds are guaranteed by the state.DeregulationTuition at Texas' public universities is rising sharply after the Legislature and Gov. Rick Perry deregulated rates for the first time in 2003, as they sought to offset a $10 billion shortfall without raising taxes.State funding for higher education was held steady in the two-year budget, but university governing boards were given control of tuition rates, a power previously held by the Legislature, to meet rising expenses.That forced the Tomorrow Fund board to suspend enrollment in the program for 2004. Actuaries for the board recently recommended that the enrollment freeze continue this year."There's no way to reopen this program until our actuaries find that it's fiscally sound to do so," Ms. Strayhorn said.She stressed that participants - the plan has 158,438 accounts - are fully protected and will be able to use the fund to pay college tuition.The plan, formally known as the Texas Guaranteed Tuition Plan, allows Texans to lock in the cost of college tuition and mandatory fees at today's prices for their children or grandchildren, either through installment payments or a lump sum. Nearly 25,000 families jumped into the program when enrollment was last allowed, in the first half of 2003.Ms. Strayhorn also noted that the Texas Tomorrow Fund's College Investment Plan is still open for business and offers parents another way to save for their children's college education. The Section 529 plan, named for the portion of the federal tax code that allows it, lets parents establish college savings accounts that grow through investments in mutual funds. The earnings are not subject to federal taxes."Our 529 plan

e on our tuition and the way our institutions are using their authority to set tuition rates," Ms. Shapiro said. A WALL STREET JOisRNAL News Roundup Wall Street Journal (1923 - Current file); Apr 16, 1987; ProQuest Historical Newspapers: The Texaco Inc. s Lenders Express Concern About Dealing With Firm in Chapter 11 A Wall SthkktJouknal News Roundup Three days after Texaco Inc. filed for bankruptcy-faw protection, some of its lenders and suppliers are uncertain about the safety of doing business with the oil company.. Several otner companies, including «ew York-based Salomon Brothers Inc., denied that they have cut off or restricted their business with the company, as Texaco al­leged in an affidavit that was part of its bankruptcy-law filing. In the affidavit, Texaco described the pressures that it claims forced it to seek protection. Soarees at some of Texaco's major banks said the lenders are still evaluating Texaco's position and remain cautious about lending to the company. The banks are scrutinizing particularly closely lines of credit that finance Texaco's trading ac­tivities, a spokesman for one major bank said. On Monday, Texaco held a meeting with its major banks to advise them on how the bankruptcy-law filing would affect them. Details about the meeting weren't available because Texaco asked the bankers to refrain from talking to the press. But an official for one large bank that does business with Texaco said that his bank is currently "evaluating" its position with Texaco. However, he added that "there's no liquidity crisis at Texaco." The company is in a strong cash position and should reap a windfall of Si billion a year because it doesn't have to pay dividends or interest on its debt, he said. Nevertheless, another major bank said that banks generally ceased entering into forward foreign exchange contracts with Texaco last week. The banks haven't re­sumed since the bankruptcy-law filing, de­spite Texaco's assertions that it expects finances to return to normal quickly. Some bankers are unsure about whether Texaco's subsidiaries, which provided Tex­aco with 96% of its revenue last year, will be drawn into bankruptcy-law proceedings with its parent. Currently they are ex­cluded. Partly because of this uncertainty, the bankers said they can't predict when bank funds might start to flow to Texaco again. Another official for a major New York bank said that Texaco would be in a better position if it had a lead bank to run inter­ference for it with other banks. "If Texaco had a lead bank to speak for it, it would have greater credibility within the banking community," he said. Some of Texaco's suppliers also remain skittish. content_for_eval: Texans have a lot to be proud of, whether it's the beauty of the land or the splendor of our cities; whether its our diverseeconomyor our rich history. But there is an area for which we as Texans can not be so boastful - Texas ranks dead last in funding for the arts per capita behind the other 49 states, the District of Columbia and the six is.S. territories. Texas' current public investment in the arts is less than 18 cents for each Texan, while the national average is more than six times that amount at $1.12 per citizen.In 1993, the Texas Legislature established the Texas Cultural Endowment Fund, the first of its kind in the nation. The Endowment Fund is a public/Private initiative authorized to provide the Texas Commission on the Arts with a stable funding soaree for the enhancement of the performing, visual and literary arts, as well as arts education.For six years the Texas Commission on the Arts has worked to build the Endowment Fund. The organization currently operates on an annual budget of $5.3 million. Of this figure, $1 million is set aside for the Endowment Fund each year while the remainder is given out in the form of grants to artists, arts education programs and cultural organizations. The Endowment Fund has approximately $7.5 million dollars in it at the present time.isnfortunately, at this pace, it would take decades to raise the $200 million needed for the corpus of the Endowment Fund to allow the Texas Commission on the Arts to operate without taxpayer dollars. That is why the Commission is requesting a $200-million interest-free loan from the state for the purpose of realizing the Texas Cultural Endowment Fund.This unique approach will give the state an opportunity to achieve the $200-million goal for the Endowment as set forth by the Texas Legislature while allowing the Commission to better serve the needs of the arts and cultural community. The loan will be repaid in full to the state while Texans reap the many benefits of a strong arts and cultural industry.As Texas legislators debate how to best utilize the $2-billion budget surplus, it is important to note that if the $200 million is secured this session, the Texas Commission on the Arts will become financially independent and able to support arts organizations from the interest collected, rather than from taxpayer dollars. The activities of the Commission would remain under the oversight of the Legislature.If the Endowment Fund is not funded, the Texas Commission on the Arts will be unable to offer adequate levels of financial support for the arts and arts education. Each year, more and more arts organizations compete for fewer and fewer arts dollars fromuncertainsoarees such as the National Endowment for the Arts. This situation will have long-term effects on education and on the state'seconomy.Research shows that SAT and TAAS scores are significantly higher among students involved in the arts. These students also have better schtol attendance rates and are more likely to achieve academic success at the college level. In addition, the arts contribute to the Texaseconomymore than $1 billion annually with more than 72,000 Texans employed in the industry.What's more, tourism is Texas' third largest industry, pumping $27.5 million into the state'seconomyin 1996. Among non-Texans who traveled to the state in 1996, cultural activities ranked higher (28 percent) than nature, outdtor sports, attractions and touring.Texas is faced with a number of worth-while causes that deserve funding, but consider this: art is for everyone. Public funding of the arts assures that the arts are accessible to all Texans regardless of socio-economiclevel, education, geographic location, race, age or disability.Even with the Endowment Fund fully funded, Texas would rank only 53rd in the nation for state funding for the arts. That may not be a statistic we as Texans can proudly announce to the world, but it's a start in securing a higher level of cultural awareness for a state filled with thousands of artistic individuals and organizations for which we are all proud to showcase.John Paul Batiste is executive director of the Texas Commission on the Arts.
